← All UPSC / Banking material

UPSC / Banking · Reference source

Economic Survey 2025

12 sections summarised · 360 dedicated questions

01

State of the Economy & Growth Outlook

State of the Economy & Growth Outlook – Economic Survey 2025

  • Growth potential & near‑term outlook
    • Potential real‑GDP growth revised to 7.0 % (up from 6.5 % three years earlier).
    • “Full‑year” growth > 7 % is projected for FY 2026 and is expected to stay at a similar level in FY 2027.
    • The Survey’s now‑casting model (dynamic‑factor, 17 high‑frequency indicators) gives a 7 % estimate for Q3 FY 26 (RMSE 0.87).
    • Medium‑term projection (Box I.4) puts real‑GDP growth at ≈ 7 % for the 2025‑27 horizon.
  • Latest advance estimates (H1 FY 26)
    • Real‑GDP growth (first‑advance): 7.4 %.
    • GVA growth: 7.3 %; services‑GVA share 53.6 % of GDP.
    • Private final consumption expenditure (PFCE): 61.5 % of GDP – highest share since FY 12; PFCE grew 7.5 % YoY in H1 FY 26.
    • Gross fixed capital formation (GFCF): 30 % of GDP (FAE); grew 7.6 % YoY (≈ ₹10.5 L cr in FY 20‑25).
  • Fiscal stance – Union
    • General‑government deficit (FY 26): 4.8 % of GDP (budgeted 4.9 %).
    • Target for FY 26: 4.4 % of GDP, fulfilling the 2021 promise to cut the deficit by more than half from 9.2 % in FY 21.
    • Primary deficit (FD − interest) is now ≈ 0 % (revenues cover non‑interest spending).
    • Debt‑to‑GDP anchor: 50 % ± 1 % by FY 31 (medium‑term fiscal anchor).
  • Fiscal stance – States
    • Rising revenue deficits and unconditional cash‑transfer programmes in several states are “crowding out” capital‑forming expenditure (Box II‑6).
  • Public‑investment intensity
    • General‑government investment: 4 % of GDP (FY 24) – comparable with OECD middle‑income peers (2‑5 %).
    • Despite fiscal consolidation, a record level of public investment has been achieved; however, state‑level fiscal pressures are flagged as emerging risks.
  • External sector
    • Exports FY 25 (record): US$ 825.3 bn (goods + services).
    • Exports FY 26 (goods + services): +5.9 % YoY (Apr‑Dec FY 26).
    • Services‑export performance (2020‑25): CAGR 9.4 % (services‑heavy); Merchandise‑export CAGR 6.4 %.
    • Services‑export share: 9.7 % of GDP (FY 23‑25) → ≈ 10 % (H1 FY 26).
    • Current‑account deficit: 0.8 % of GDP (H1 FY 26) – down from 1.3 % (H1 FY 25).
    • Remittances (private transfers): US$ 73 bn in H1 FY 26 (↑ 13 % YoY).
    • Foreign‑direct investment (FDI): +16.1 % YoY (Apr‑Nov FY 25).
  • Trade policy & market access
    • Free‑Trade Agreement with the EU concluded after three years of negotiations; pending ratification by the European Parliament – expands market access for labour‑intensive manufactured exports and deepens technology integration.
    • Sector‑specific FDI openings: nuclear power generation opened to the private sector; insurance sector permitted 100 % foreign‑direct investment.
  • Government borrowing costs
    • 10‑yr sovereign bond yield: 6.7 % (BBB rating).
    • Indonesia (BBB) yields 6.3 %, indicating a state‑level fiscal shadow on India’s sovereign borrowing costs.
  • Credit‑rating upgrades (2025)
    • Morningstar DBRS – upgrade (May 2025).
    • S&P – upgraded from BBB‑ to BBB (August 2025) – first major upgrade by a global agency in ~20 years.
    • R&I – upgrade (September 2025).
    • Narrative: upgrades signal improved perception of fiscal consolidation, revenue buoyancy, growth‑enhancing capex and greater fiscal transparency.
  • Cost of capital & external balances
    • Persistent current‑account deficits generate a risk premium on foreign savings, keeping the cost of capital elevated.
    • The Survey stresses a structural goal: shift to a surplus‑generating economy to lower the cost of capital durably.
  • Monetary policy & liquidity
    • RBI cut policy rates aggressively in early 2025; cumulative repo‑rate cuts ≈ 125 bps (Feb‑Nov 2025) bringing the repo to 5.25 % by Dec 2025.
    • Macro‑prudential measures (2023) were relaxed as conditions improved (CRR cut 100 bps to 3 %, OMO injections).
    • Liquidity‑adjustment facility (LAF) surplus ₹1.89 L cr (FY 26) – ample buffer.
  • Money supply & financial intermediation (as of 31 Dec 2025)
    • Reserve money (M₀) growth: 2.9 % YoY (vs 4.9 % Dec 2024).
    • Broad money (M₃) growth: 12.1 % YoY; money multiplier 6.21 (up from 5.70).
    • Bank credit to commercial sector: +14.1 % YoY (Dec 2025).
  • Banking‑sector health (Sept 2025)
    • CRAR (capital‑to‑risk‑weighted assets): 17.2 %.
    • GNPA at a multi‑decadal low (exact % not quoted); NPA recovery 26.2 % FY 25.
    • Weighted‑average lending rate (fresh rupee loans): 8.71 % (down 64 bps).
  • External financing buffers
    • Foreign‑exchange reserves cover > 11 months of imports (as of 16 Jan 2026) and ≈ 94 % of external debt (Sept 2025).
    • India ranks among the lower‑risk group of the world’s 25 biggest sovereign debtors (exact rank not given).
  • Infrastructure & logistics
    • Airport network doubled over the past decade.
    • Freight movement via inland waterways has grown rapidly, easing logistics bottlenecks.
    • Power‑Gap Index (Lowy Institute): ‑4.0, the lowest score in Asia (excluding Russia & North Korea).
  • Rupee valuation
    • Survey describes the rupee as “punching below its weight” – undervalued relative to strong fundamentals; this cushions India from higher import‑tariff impacts and curbs inflation from expensive crude imports.
  • Global scenario probabilities for 2026 (Survey’s geopolitical outlook)

1. Best‑case (managed disorder) – ≈ 40‑45 % probability.

2. Disorderly multipolar breakdown – ≈ 40‑45 % probability.

3. Systemic shock cascade – ≈ 10‑20 % probability.

  • High‑frequency indicators (HFI)
    • Point to robust domestic demand, firming investment momentum and strengthening industrial activity (Charts 1‑20).
  • Regulatory and structural reforms affecting growth
    • GST: “Most radical overhaul of the Goods and Services Tax since its inception in 2017” announced in the FY 26 Budget (intended to broaden the tax base and lower compliance costs).
    • Labour reforms: Four Labour Codes notified (details to be framed in the next few months).
    • Environmental‑regulation shift: Uniform 33 % green‑cover norm replaced by a pollution‑potential‑based norm.
    • Quality‑Control Orders: “Indiscriminate Quality Control Orders … were put on hold”.
  • Key take‑aways for examinations

1. Growth outlook now anchored at ≈ 7 % potential (up from 6.5 %).

2. Fiscal deficit halved to ≈ 4.8 % (target 4.4 % FY 26).

3. Credit‑rating upgrades (S&P, DBRS, R&I) after a two‑decade hiatus.

4. External sector: large trade surplus in services (CAGR 9.4 %) offsets goods‑trade deficit; CAD down to 0.8 % of GDP.

5. RBI policy stance: repo at 5.25 %, ample liquidity, falling borrowing costs.

6. Public‑investment intensity ≈ 4 % of GDP – record level alongside fiscal consolidation.

7. Infrastructure boost: doubled airports, thriving inland‑waterway freight, low Power‑Gap score.

8. Rupee undervaluation and strong reserves act as buffers against external shocks.

These bullet points compile every fact from the Economic Survey 2025 that directly describes the state of the economy and the growth outlook, preserving exact figures, percentages, scheme names, targets, years, institutional references and definitions required for RBI‑Grade B / UPSC General‑Awareness preparation.

02

Fiscal Developments & Public Finance

Fiscal Developments & Public Finance – Economic Survey 2025‑26

All figures, percentages, scheme names, targets and institutional references are reproduced exactly from the Survey notes.

1. Overall fiscal stance

  • General‑government deficit (FY 26 / 2025‑26) – 4.8 % of GDP (budgeted 4.9 %).
  • Target for FY 26 – 4.4 % of GDP, fulfilling the 2021 promise to cut the deficit by more than half from 9.2 % in FY 21.
  • Fiscal deficit trajectory (Centre)
    • FY 21 : 9.2 % → FY 22 : 7.3 % → FY 23 : 6.0 % → FY 24 : 5.0 % → FY 25 (PA) : 4.8 % → FY 26 (BE) : 4.4 %.
  • Primary deficit (FY 25 PA) – 0.4 % of GDP (virtually zero; interest payments cover the rest).
  • Revenue deficit (FY 25 PA) – 1.0 % of GDP.
  • Debt sustainability – the Survey finds the primary surplus rises as debt accumulates (a debt-sustainable path in the Bohn (1998) sense), and that the fiscal stance is counter-cyclical.

2. Revenue side

IndicatorFY 25 / FY 26 (as quoted)Comment
Revenue receipts (share of GDP)≈ 9.1 % (FY 22‑25 PA)↑ from ≈ 8.5 % (FY 16‑20)
Gross tax revenue (share of GDP)11.5 % (FY 25 PA)↑ from 10.8 %
Direct‑tax share of total taxes58.8 % (FY 25 PA)Up from 51.9 % (pre‑pandemic)
Non‑corporate taxes (personal income, STT, etc.)3.3 % of GDP (FY 25 PA)Buoyancy ≈ 1.8 (FY 23‑25 PA)
Corporate‑tax collections₹9.80 lakh cr (FY 25) vs ₹4.58 lakh cr (FY 21)Listed‑company profits doubled (₹2.5 tr → ₹7.1 tr)
GST receipts (Apr‑Dec 2025)₹17.4 lakh cr, +6.7 % YoYTax‑payer base grew from 60 lakh (2017) → > 1.5 crore (2025)
GST‑2.0 rate structure (effective 22‑Sep‑2025)– Standard 18 %
– Merit 5 %
– “Sin‑good” 40 %
– Major cuts: tractors 5 % (from 12 %), fertiliser acids 5 % (from 18 %), small cars 18 % (from 28 %), auto‑parts 18 % (from 28 %), electronics 18 % (from 28 %), man‑made fibres 5 % (from 18 %)
– Zero GST on UHT milk, paneer & essential foods; 5 % on most packaged foods; 0 % on life‑saving drugs.
Excise duty – fell from 1.7 % (FY 22) → 0.9 % (FY 25) (petroleum‑rate cuts).
Customs‑duty buoyancy – 0.4 (moderate) after raw‑material rate cuts.

3. Expenditure profile

  • Effective capital expenditure (Capex + grants) – 4 % of GDP (FY 25 PA); post‑pandemic average 3.9 % → 4 %.
  • Capital expenditure (capex alone) – 2.9 % of GDP (post‑pandemic average).
  • Revenue expenditure – 10.9 % of GDP (FY 25) (down from 13.6 % (FY 22); below pre‑pandemic 11.1 %.
  • Major subsidies – 1.2 % of GDP (FY 25) → 1.1 % (FY 26 BE).
  • Public‑investment intensity (FY 24) – General‑government investment = 4 % of GDP (≈ 1/5 of total revenue); comparable with OECD middle‑income peers (2‑5 %).
  • Capital spending as share of central outlay (FY 20‑25) – 22.6 % (record public investment).
  • Capital‑spending as share of GDP (FY 25) – 4.0 %.

3.1 Targeted spending programmes

  • GST radical overhaul announced in FY 26 Budget (most extensive since 2017).
  • Unconditional cash‑transfer programmes (states) – projected ₹1.7 lakh cr (FY 26); 0.19‑1.25 % of GSDP; 0.68‑8.26 % of total budget – flagged as “expenditure rigidity” that may raise sovereign‑bond yields.

4. Public‑Financial‑Management (PFM) reforms

ReformKey metrics (as of FY 26)
Just‑in‑Time (JIT) fund releases via SNA‑SPARSH (CSS) & Treasury Single Account (TSA) / TSA‑Hybrid• Idle balances fell: ₹1.67 lakh cr (Apr 2024) → ₹0.4 lakh cr (15 Jan 2026).
• TSA assignments ₹3.09 lakh cr issued; ₹2.45 lakh cr spent (FY 26).
Direct‑Benefit‑Transfer (DBT) leak‑reductionSaved ₹3.48 lakh cr over the last decade; beneficiary coverage ↑ 16‑fold (≈ 11 cr → 176 cr).
Non‑tax receipts – stable at ≈ 1.4 % of GDP.
Dividends & profits – CPSE dividends ₹0.72 lakh cr → ₹1.39 lakh cr (‑69 % → +69 % FY 25).
• RBI surplus transfer ₹2.68 lakh cr (FY 25) (+ 27 % YoY).
Disinvestment & equity monetisation (FY 26)• OFS of Mazagon Dock, Bank of Maharashtra & Indian Overseas Bank → ₹7,717 cr.
• SUUTI remittances ₹1,051 cr.
• InvIT monetisation ₹18,837 cr.
• Strategic disinvestment: 36 CPSEs approved, 13 completed (FY 21‑26).
• Proposal: re‑define “government company” (minimum 26 % central share) to deepen equity monetisation.
Centre‑State fiscal transfers• Share of GDP rose 5.7 % → 6.9 % (FY 20 → FY 26).
• Absolute value ₹11.5 lakh cr → ₹25.6 lakh cr.
15th Finance Commission (FY 26) grants – total ₹1.48 lakh cr broken down into revenue‑deficit, local‑body, health, disaster‑management components.
Special Assistance to States for Capital Investment (SASCI)• 50‑yr interest‑free loans for state capex.
• Uptake ₹4.50 lakh cr (FY 21‑26).
• FY 26 allocation ₹150,000 cr – ₹68,000 cr untied, ₹80,000 cr reform‑linked, ₹2,000 cr for fiscally‑strained states.
• State capex (incl. SASCI) 2.17 % → 2.37 % of GDP (FY 22 → FY 25 PA); excl. SASCI 2.11 % → 1.92 %, underscoring SASCI’s stabilising role.
State‑level debt & borrowing• Debt‑to‑GSDP (all 28 states) – 28.1 % (FY 25 PA).
• Interest‑payments‑to‑Revenue‑Receipts ≈ 12.6 %.
• Net borrowing ceiling (XV‑FC, FY 26) – 3 % of GSDP (+ 0.5 % for power sector).
Unconditional cash‑transfer programmes (states) – projected outlay ₹1.7 lakh cr (FY 26), representing 0.19‑1.25 % of GSDP and 0.68‑8.26 % of total budget.
State‑level revenue deficits – 0.1 % (FY 19) → 0.7 % (FY 25 PA) of GDP; overall state fiscal deficit rose 2.6 % → 3.2 % of GDP (FY 22‑25 PA).

5. Debt & financing

ParameterFigure / Trend
10‑yr sovereign bond yield (FY 26)6.7 % (India, BBB rating).
Comparative BBB‑rated peer – Indonesia 10‑yr yield 6.3 % → “state‑level fiscal shadow” on sovereign borrowing costs.
Credit‑rating upgrades (2025)• Morningstar DBRS – May 2025.
• S&P – August 2025 upgraded from BBB‑ → BBB (first major upgrade in ~20 years).
• R&I – September 2025.
Debt‑to‑GDP target (central) – 50 % ± 1 % by FY 31 (medium‑term anchor).
General‑government debt – fell by ≈ 7.1 pp since 2020 (outperforming many peers).
External debt – ≈ 2.6 % of GDP, largely concessional multilateral borrowing.
Outstanding debt coupon – down 7.71 % (FY 20) → 7.25 % (FY 25).
Weighted‑average maturity (WAM) of fresh FY 26 issuances – ≈ 19.1 years (FY 25 ≈ 20.7 yr).
Weighted‑average coupon (WAC) of fresh FY 26 securities – 6.65 % (FY 25 ≈ 7.11 %).
Rollover risk – only 27 % of outstanding debt matures in next 5 years; average annual rollover ≈ 5.4 %.
Floating‑rate debt – 4.1 % of the stock (end‑FY 24).
External‑debt‑to‑GDP – 18.4 % (FY 24), well below many large economies.
NIIP (Net International Investment Position) – ‑USD 0.3 tn (11th largest global debtor); NIIP‑to‑assets ratio improved 74.1 % → 77.5 % (Mar 24 → Mar 25).
Exchange‑rate elasticity – 1 % rupee appreciation cuts merchandise trade by ‑1.45 %; services trade less elastic (‑0.38 %).

6. External sector & cost of capital (relevant to public finance)

  • Current‑account deficit (H1 FY 26) – 0.8 % of GDP (down from 1.3 % in H1 FY 25).
  • Exports (goods + services) – USD 825.3 bn FY 25 record; FY 26 goods + services +5.9 % YoY.
  • Trade‑related cost of capital – Persistent current‑account deficits create a risk premium on foreign savings, keeping sovereign borrowing costs high; the Survey targets a surplus‑generating economy to lower the cost of capital durably.
  • External debt rankings – India among the lower‑risk group of the world’s 25 biggest debtors (exact rank not quoted).

7. Key fiscal‑policy narratives

  • “Entrepreneurial state” – emphasis on proactive, mission‑mode platforms (semiconductors, green hydrogen) financed through high‑impact public investment while maintaining fiscal consolidation.
  • Credit‑rating narrative – upgrades cited deficit reduction, revenue buoyancy, growth‑enhancing capex, fiscal transparency as the drivers of improved perception.
  • Fiscal decentralisation – Box II‑5 (not reproduced) discusses fiscal devolution and economic convergence; rising state revenue deficits and unconditional cash‑transfer programmes are flagged as crowding‑out capital‑forming expenditure.
  • Radical GST overhaul – announced in FY 26 Budget; the Survey calls it “the most radical overhaul of GST since its inception in 2017”.

8. Summary of quantitative targets (as of FY 26)

TargetValue
Fiscal deficit4.4 % of GDP (FY 26)
Primary deficit≈ 0 % (FY 25 PA)
Revenue deficit1.0 % of GDP (FY 25 PA)
Debt‑to‑GDP (central)≤ 50 % ± 1 % by FY 31
General‑government debt reduction≈ 7.1 pp since 2020
General‑government investment4 % of GDP (FY 24)
Capital expenditure (capex + grants)4 % of GDP (FY 25 PA)
Major subsidies≈ 1.1 % of GDP (FY 26 BE)
Centre‑State transfers6.9 % of GDP (FY 26)
SASCI allocation FY 26₹150,000 cr (₹68,000 cr untied, ₹80,000 cr reform‑linked, ₹2,000 cr for fiscally‑strained states)
External debt≈ 2.6 % of GDP
10‑yr bond yield6.7 % (BBB rating)
Credit‑rating upgradesMorningstar DBRS (May 2025), S&P (Aug 2025 to BBB), R&I (Sep 2025)

All points above are drawn exclusively from the Economic Survey 2025‑26 excerpts. No additional assumptions or invented data have been introduced.

03

Monetary & Financial Sector Developments

Monetary & Financial‑Sector Developments (Economic Survey 2025‑26)

  • Monetary‑policy stance & RBI actions
    • Repo‑rate cut 125 bps (Feb 2025 – Nov 2025); cumulative cut 100 bps in FY 26, leaving the policy repo at 5.25 % (Dec 2025).
    • Cash‑Reserve‑Ratio (CRR) reduced by 100 bps to 3.0 % of NDTL, releasing ≈ ₹2.5 lakh cr of liquidity.
    • Open‑Market Operations (OMO):
    • ₹2.39 lakh cr (Apr‑May 2025) + ₹1 lakh cr (Dec 2025).
    • Foreign‑exchange swap: USD 5 bn 3‑yr swap (Dec 2025) to hedge external financing costs.
    • Macro‑prudential easing: 2023 macro‑prudential measures relaxed as market conditions improved.
  • Liquidity‑adjustment facilities (RBI)
    • Liquidity‑Adjustment Facility (LAF) net surplus ₹1.89 lakh cr (FY 26) vs ₹0.16 lakh cr FY 25.
    • Marginal‑Standing Facility (MSF) borrowing fell to ₹2,244 cr (down from ₹6,902 cr FY 25).
    • Standing‑Deposit Facility (SDF) deployment rose to ₹1.74 lakh cr (up from ₹0.94 lakh cr FY 25).
    • Weighted‑average call rate stayed 8 bps below the repo rate, keeping short‑term funding cheap.
  • Monetary aggregates (as of 31 Dec 2025)
    • Reserve money (M₀) growth 2.9 % YoY (vs 4.9 % Dec 2024).
    • CRR‑adjusted M₀ growth 9.4 % YoY (vs 6.2 % prior year).
    • Currency in circulation ↑ 10.2 % YoY.
    • Broad money (M₃) growth 12.1 % YoY (vs 9 % previous).
    • Bank deposits +12.3 % YoY.
    • Bank credit to commercial sector +14.1 % YoY.
    • Money multiplier (M₃/M₀) 6.21 (up from 5.70); adjusted for reverse‑repo/SDF ≈ 6.0.
  • Transmission to banking sector
    • Weighted‑average lending rate (WALR) – fresh rupee loans fell 64 bps to 8.71 % (Nov 2025).
    • WALR – outstanding rupee loans fell 56 bps to 9.21 % (Nov 2025) – lowest since Sep 2022.
    • Call‑rate spread remained ≈ 8 bps below the repo, indicating strong transmission.
  • Banking‑sector health (SCBs)
    • CRAR 17.2 % (latest available).
    • GNPA (gross NPA) ratio at a multi‑decadal low (exact % not quoted).
    • Sectoral GNPA (Mar 2025):
    • Industry 1.9 % (down from 2.3 %).
    • Services 1.8 % (down from 2.0 %).
    • Personal loans 1.1 % (down from 1.2 %).
    • Agriculture 6.0 % (share of GNPA 36.3 %).
    • NPA‑recovery rate 26.2 % FY 25 (up from 13.2 % FY 18).
    • Slippage ratio 1.3 % FY 26 (down from 1.4 % FY 25).
    • IBC‑recovery rate 36.6 % FY 25 (up from 28.3 % FY 24).
    • SARFESI‑recovery rate 31.5 % FY 25 (up from 25.4 % FY 24).
    • PAT growth +16.9 % YoY FY 25; +3.8 % YoY Sep 25.
    • ROE 13.6 % (Mar 2025); ROA 1.3 % (Mar 2025).
  • Credit‑growth (scheduled commercial banks)
    • Total credit growth +14.5 % YoY (Dec 2025) vs +11.2 % Dec 2024.
    • Non‑food credit +58.6 % YoY (Dec 2025).
    • Personal‑loan credit +12.8 % YoY (Nov 2025); gold‑loan surge +125.3 % YoY.
    • MSME credit +21.8 % YoY (Nov 2025) (vs +13 % Nov 2024).
    • Micro‑enterprise credit +24.6 % YoY.
  • Regional Rural Banks (RRBs) – consolidation from 196 → 28 entities (1 May 2025).
    • Net profit FY 25 ₹6.8 k cr (vs ₹7.6 k cr FY 24).
    • GNPA 5.4 % (FY 25) (down from 6.1 % FY 24) – lowest in 13 years.
    • Credit‑to‑deposit ratio 73.8 % (Mar 2025) (↑ 71.4 % Mar 2024).
    • CRAR 14.4 % (FY 25).
    • Priority‑sector‑lending > 75 % of adjusted net credit met consistently.
  • Regulatory & Institutional reforms (RBI)
    • 7 May 2025 – Framework for Formulation of Regulations: transparent, consultative rule‑making; periodic impact‑analysis and review.
    • 28 Nov 2025 – Consolidated Master Directions: 9,446 circulars repealed; 238 Master Directions retained (3,809 incorporated, 5,673 obsolete).
    • 1 Oct 2025 – Regulatory Review Cell operational; each regulation reviewed every 5‑7 years.
    • Advisory Group on Regulation (established 2025) – external experts for continuous stakeholder engagement.
    • AI Governance – FREE‑AI framework (2025): 7 “Sutras” (Trust, People‑First, Innovation, Fairness, Accountability, Explainability, Safety) + 6 strategic pillars; ≈ 21 % of banks/NBFCs have any AI implementation (basic chat‑bots, process‑efficiency).
  • Micro‑finance sector
    • NBFC‑MFIs hold 39 % of loan‑outstanding (vs banks 32 %, small finance banks 16 %, other NBFCs 12 %).
    • Active borrowers 627 lakh (FY 25) vs 330 lakh (FY 14).
    • Gross loan portfolio grew from ₹33,517 cr (FY 14) to ₹2,38,198 cr (FY 25).

Branch network expanded from 11,687 (FY 14) to 37,380 (FY 25).

  • FY 25 loan‑outstanding fell ‑14 % YoY (post‑pandemic demand shock).
    • Qualifying‑asset rule lowered (June 2025) from 75 % → 60 % of total assets, easing risk‑weighting for MFIs.
    • Self‑Regulatory Organisations (SROs) set best‑practice codes complementing RBI oversight.
  • Government‑led financial‑inclusion schemes
    • Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana (PMJDY): 55.02 crore accounts (36.63 crore rural) – foundation for savings & transactions.
    • Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojana (PMMY): ₹36.18 lakh cr disbursed; 55.45 crore loan accounts; >10 crore first‑time borrowers (Oct 2025).
    • Shishu‑loan share fell from 92 % → 63 %; average Shishu ticket ≈ ₹1 lakh (doubled).
    • Tarun‑loan average ticket > ₹7 lakh.
    • PM Street‑Vendor Atmanirbhar Nidhi (PM SVANidhi): 5,130 beneficiaries (99 ULBs, FY 25); 33 % women; outcomes – income growth +20 % (FY 23‑25); housing upgrade 39 %, food access 55 %, health 44 %, education 50 %.
    • Financial Inclusion Index (FI‑Index) – composite score 67.0 (Mar 2025) vs 64.2 (Mar 2024); all sub‑indices (access, usage, quality) improved.
  • Digital payments & financial deepening
    • UPI used for ≥ 60 % of transaction categories; ≥ 80 % of users employ it for 3+ purposes (retail, P2P, bill‑pay, e‑commerce).
    • Digital‑payment usage rose from 45 % of adults (2023) to 83 % (2025).
    • Account‑Aggregator (AA) framework supplying lenders with verified bank‑transaction, GST and other data, expanding credit to first‑time borrowers.
  • Debt‑management (central government)
    • Weighted‑average maturity (WAM) of fresh FY 26 issuances ≈ 19.1 yr (down from 20.7 yr FY 25).
    • Weighted‑average coupon (WAC) of FY 26 securities 6.65 % (down from 7.11 % FY 25).
    • Outstanding‑debt coupon fell from 7.71 % (FY 20) to 7.25 % (FY 25).
    • Rollover risk: only 27 % of outstanding debt matures in next 5 years; average annual rollover ≈ 5.4 %.
    • Floating‑rate debt 4.1 % of stock (end‑FY 24).
    • External debt ≈ 2.6 % of GDP, largely concessional multilateral borrowing.
  • External financing buffers
    • Foreign‑exchange reserves cover > 11 months of imports (as of 16 Jan 2026); 94 % of external debt covered (Sept 25).
    • Rupee valuation described as “punching below its weight” – undervalued relative to fundamentals, cushioning India from higher import‑tariff impacts and curbing inflation from expensive crude imports.
  • Sovereign‑credit‑rating upgrades (2025)
    • Morningstar DBRS – upgrade (May 2025).
    • S&P – upgraded from BBB‑ to BBB (Aug 2025) – first major upgrade by a large agency in ~20 years.
    • R&I – upgrade (Sept 2025).
    • Result: lower sovereign‑bond spreads, 10‑yr bond yield 6.7 % (BBB rating).
  • Capital‑expenditure & fiscal context supporting finance
    • Record public investment achieved alongside fiscal consolidation; capital‑expenditure (capex + grants) ≈ 4 % of GDP (FY 25 PA).
    • “State‑level fiscal shadow” noted: 10‑yr bond yield 6.7 % (India) vs 6.3 % (Indonesia) despite same BBB rating, underscoring the impact of sub‑national fiscal health on sovereign borrowing costs.
  • Capital markets – primary‑market mobilisation (brief)
    • Nifty up +11.1 % (Apr‑Dec 2025).
    • Total primary‑market mobilisation ₹10.7 lakh cr (debt + equity) till Dec 2025.

These points capture all monetary‑policy moves, liquidity management, banking‑sector health, micro‑finance, financial‑inclusion programmes, regulatory reforms, debt‑management, external buffers and sovereign‑rating developments recorded in the Economic Survey 2025‑26.

04

External Sector: Trade, Balance of Payments & Forex

External‑Sector Highlights (Economic Survey 2025‑26)

1. Trade – Merchandise (Goods)

  • Export performance
    • FY 2025 total exports (goods + services) = US$ 825.3 bn – a record, +6.1 % YoY.
    • FY 2025 merchandise exports = US$ 437.7 bn (steady vs FY 2024).
    • Non‑petroleum, non‑gems‑jewellery items made up 78.7 % of merchandise exports and grew 7.5 % YoY; non‑petroleum export value hit a record US$ 374.3 bn.
    • Key export commodities FY 25
    • Petroleum products – ‑24.7 % (price fell 15.4 % YoY).
    • Telecom instruments + 51.2 %.
    • Drug formulations & biologics + 11.2 %.
  • Import performance
    • FY 2025 merchandise imports = US$ 721.2 bn (+6.3 % YoY).
    • Non‑petroleum, non‑gems‑jewellery imports = US$ 446.5 bn.
    • Crude oil, gold and petroleum products together accounted for > ⅓ of total imports.
    • Gold imports + 27.4 % YoY; gold price + 38.2 % YoY.
  • Trade balance
    • FY 2025 merchandise trade deficit = US$ 283.5 bn (+17.6 % YoY).
    • Overall (goods + services) trade deficit = US$ 94.7 bn (FY 25).
  • Sector‑wise export growth (PLI‑linked)
    • PLI‑export AAGR (FY 21‑25) = 10.6 %.
    • PLI‑import AAGR (FY 21‑25) = 12.6 %.
    • Notable sector AAGRs (exports): IT‑hardware +77.2 %, ACC batteries +45.0 %, electronics +38.8 %, solar PV +23.9 %.
  • Agricultural‑product exports
    • CAGR FY 20‑25 = 8.2 % (USD 34.5 bn → USD 51.1 bn).
    • Share of agri‑exports in merchandise exports = 11–14 % (FY 20‑25).
    • Target: US$ 100 bn agri‑/marine‑/food‑beverage‑exports in the next 4 years.

2. Trade – Services

  • Exports
    • FY 2025 services exports = US$ 387.5 bn (+13.6 % YoY) – all‑time high.
    • FY 2026 (Apr‑Dec) services exports = US$ 304.0 bn (+6.5 % YoY).
  • Imports
    • FY 2025 services imports = US$ 198.7 bn (+11.4 % YoY).
    • FY 2026 (Apr‑Dec) services imports = US$ 152.2 bn (+1.5 % YoY).
  • Surplus
    • FY 2025 services surplus = US$ 188.8 bn (≈ 2/3 of the merchandise deficit).
    • FY 2026 (Apr‑Dec) services surplus = US$ 151.7 bn (61 % of the merchandise deficit).
  • Composition
    • Business services ≈ 48 % of total services exports; software services ≈ 49 % (stable FY 25‑26).
    • Market shares – US share fell to 52.9 % (from 54.1 %); Europe share rose to 32.8 %.
  • Growth drivers
    • Services‑export CAGR (2020‑25) = 9.4 % (overall), 6.4 % for merchandise exports.
    • AI‑intensive services enjoyed a ≈ 39.5 % higher export growth (software + 44 %, business services + 67 %).

3. Balance‑of‑Payments (BoP) – Current Account

PeriodCurrent‑account deficit% of GDPRemarks
H1 FY 25US$ 25.3 bn1.3 %
H1 FY 26US$ 15 bn0.8 %
FY 25 (full year)US$ 0.8 % of GDP
RemittancesUS$ 73 bn (H1 FY 26, +13 % YoY)FY 25 cumulative remittances US$ 135.4 bn ≈ 3.5 % of GDP. Top sources FY 25: US 27.7 %, UAE 19.2 %, UK 10.8 %, Singapore 6.6 %.
  • Net external position – Remittances exceeded net FDI inflows in FY 25, giving a stable external stance despite a goods‑trade deficit.

4. Capital Account – FDI & External Debt

  • Gross FDI inflows
    • FY 25 = US$ 81.0 bn (+13 % YoY vs US$ 71.3 bn FY 24).
    • Net FDI (Apr‑Nov FY 25) = US$ 5.6 bn (outflows = US$ 22.1 bn).
  • Sectoral composition (FY 25)
    • Equity inflows = US$ 51 bn; ≈ 60 % went into services (computer software & hardware, trading, non‑conventional energy, construction, automobiles).
  • Return on inbound FDI (2014‑2023 avg.) = 7.3 % (higher than Thailand 7 %, Brazil 6.1 %, Mexico 4.3 %).
    • Risk‑adjusted return rank = 2nd globally (after Indonesia), ratio > 7.
  • External debt (Dec 2024)
    • US$ 718.2 bn, up 10.7 % YoY.
    • External‑debt‑to‑GDP = 18.4 % (well below many large economies).
    • External debt as % of total government debt < 5 %.
    • NIIP (Net International Investment Position) – ‑US$ 0.3 tn (11th largest global debtor). NIIP ratio (assets / liabilities) improved from 74.1 % (Mar 2024) to 77.5 % (Mar 2025); NIIP / GDP = ‑8.7 % (up from ‑10.1 %).
  • External‑debt ranking – 21st worldwide, 11th among G20 (Dec 2024).

5. Foreign‑Exchange Reserves

Metric (as of 16 Jan 2026)Value
Total reservesUS$ 701.4 bn (up from US$ 668.4 bn end‑Mar 2025).
Import‑cover> 11 months of imports.
External‑debt coverage94 % of external debt (Sept 2025).
Gold componentUS$ 117.5 bn (↑ from US$ 78.2 bn).
Special Drawing Rights (SDR)US$ 18.7 bn.
IMF quotaUS$ 4.7 bn.
  • The Survey notes that the rupee is “punching below its weight” – i.e., undervalued relative to fundamentals, which cushions India from higher tariff‑induced import costs and limits inflation from expensive crude imports.

6. Exchange‑Rate Dynamics

  • Rupee movement (1 Apr 2025 – 15 Jan 2026) – ‑5.4 % vs USD (second‑largest depreciation among major Asian currencies; Japan ‑5.5 %).
  • Drivers – Persistent FPI outflows, tariff‑related trade pressures, heightened foreign‑currency demand (spot & forward).
  • Elasticities (Survey’s empirical estimates)
    • 1 % Rupee appreciation cuts net total trade by 1.26 %.
    • Merchandise‑trade elasticity: ‑1.45 % (depreciation improves trade balance).
    • Services‑trade elasticity: ‑0.38 % (relatively inelastic).
  • Debt‑Weighted Exchange‑Rate (DWER) link – 1 % appreciation raises net capital inflows 0.87 % (short‑run) but reduces them 1.02 % in the subsequent period.

7. Trade Policy & Agreements

  • Free‑Trade Agreement (FTA) with the European Union – concluded after three‑year negotiations; pending ratification by the European Parliament. Expected to expand market access for labour‑intensive manufactured exports and deepen technology/manufacturing integration.
  • Bilateral Trade / Investment Treaties (2025)
    • India‑Israel BIT (signed 2025).
    • India‑EFTA TEPA (in force Oct 2025) – binding US$ 100 bn investment commitment & 1 m jobs.
    • India‑UK CETA (concluded Jul 2025) – 137 services sub‑sectors, MRAs in nursing, accountancy, architecture; expected to lift FDI inflows.
    • India‑Oman CEPA (Dec 2025) – 127 services, 100 % FDI allowed in major services.
  • Tariff shocks – US imposed 25 % reciprocal tariff on Indian goods (Apr 2025) and an additional 25 % penal tariff (Aug 2025), effectively ≈ 50 % on many Indian merchandise exports.
  • EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) – tariffs on steel & cement based on embedded emissions, directly affecting Indian exporters.
  • RBI export‑finance measures (2025) – export‑credit tenor extended to 450 days (pre‑ and post‑shipment); export‑proceeds repatriation period stretched from 9 months → 15 months; advance‑payment shipment period extended 1 year → 3 years.

8. Global Trade Context (Survey‑wide Indicators)

Indicator (2025)Trend / Level
Friend‑shoring Index (trade‑weighted political proximity)Above 2021 average; resurgence in CY 2025 after dip in CY 2024.
Near‑shoring Index (geographic proximity)Below 2021 average; modest rise in Q3 2025.
Trade‑Concentration Index (Herfindahl)Increased in CY 2025 after a decline since CY 2024.
Global Trade‑Policy Activity (GTPA) IndexSharp rise post‑2020, driven by US‑China tensions, COVID‑19, Ukraine war and 2025 trade frictions.
TPU Index (Trade‑Policy Uncertainty) – April 2025+1,165.6 % YoY – all‑time high since 1960.
GEPU Index (Global Economic‑Policy Uncertainty) – April 2025+228.2 % YoY – record since 1997.
IMF World Economic Outlook (Oct 2025) – Global trade growthGoods & services trade growth 3.6 % in CY 2025 → 2.3 % in CY 2026 (down from 3.5 % in 2024).
Advanced economies vs EMDEs – EMDEs’ trade more vulnerable to policy uncertainty (Survey note).

These indices underscore the shift from cost‑efficiency to resilience & strategic autonomy (“geostrategic globalisation”) that frames India’s external‑sector strategy.

9. Terms‑of‑Trade (Manufacturing vs Agriculture)

  • Manufacturing‑to‑Agriculture deflator ratio – FY 25 = 0.65 (‑50 % since FY 05).
  • Manufacturing‑to‑Services deflator ratio – FY 25 = 0.81 (‑25 % since FY 05).
  • Manufacturing share in GVA (current prices) – fell from ≈ 17‑18 % (two decades ago) to ≈ 14 % (FY 25).

These ratios indicate tighter margins for factories and the need for productivity gains, reinforcing the Survey’s emphasis on moving to a surplus‑generating economy to lower the cost of capital.

10. Key Take‑aways for Exams

  • Current‑account deficit narrowed to 0.8 % of GDP (H1 FY 26); remittances now US$ 73 bn (H1 FY 26) – a major stabiliser.
  • Merchandise trade deficit remains sizable (US$ 283.5 bn FY 25) but is offset by a large services surplus.
  • Services‑export growth remains robust (+13.6 % FY 25, +6.5 % FY 26).
  • Foreign‑exchange reserves comfortably > 11 months import cover and 94 % external‑debt coverage (US$ 701 bn).
  • Rupee undervalued – “punching below its weight”, helping absorb tariff shocks.
  • FDI inflows strong (US$ 81 bn FY 25, +13 % YoY) with a 7.3 % average return, placing India 2nd in risk‑adjusted returns globally.
  • External debt modest (18.4 % of GDP, NIIP / GDP = ‑8.7 %, improving asset‑liability ratio to 77.5 %).
  • Trade‑policy environment – US 50 % tariff on many Indian goods; EU CBAM on steel & cement; pending EU‑India FTA; new BITs with Israel, EFTA, UK, Oman.
  • Export‑finance facilitation – RBI’s longer credit tenors, extended repatriation period, and longer advance‑payment windows (up to 3 years).
  • Global trade uncertainty at historic highs (TPU + 1,165 % YoY, GEPU + 228 % YoY), prompting India’s shift toward friend‑shoring and strategic autonomy.

These bullet points capture every quantitative and policy detail on the External Sector – Trade, Balance of Payments & Forex from the Economic Survey 2025‑26, ready for RBI‑Grade B / UPSC GA preparation.

05

Prices & Inflation

Economic Survey 2025 – Prices & Inflation (stand‑alone summary)

  • Headline consumer‑price inflation
    • FY 2025‑26 (Apr‑Dec 2025) – 1.7 % YoY – the lowest annual rate since the Survey began, driven by a sharp fall in food prices.
    • FY 2025 (full‑year) – 2.1 % YoY.
  • Core inflation (CPI)
    • 4.62 % (ex‑food & fuel) in Dec 2025 – the “core” component remained sticky because of spikes in precious‑metal prices.
    • ≈ 2.4 % (ex‑precious‑metals) in Dec 2025 – a falling trend as metal price pressures eased.
  • Wholesale‑price index (WPI)
    • WPI has stayed consistently below CPI throughout the year, reinforcing the overall disinflationary environment.
  • Food‑price dynamics
IndicatorFY 2025‑26 trendComments
Overall food CPIPeak‑to‑trough: Apr 2025 = 4.6 % → Sep 2025 = 1.4 % (average 2.2 %); Oct 2025 hit an all‑time low of 0.3 %.Food‑price disinflation was the main driver of the headline 1.7 % rate.
Vegetables, pulses, spicesDeflationary: ‑2 % YoY each.Lower farmgate prices and good monsoon output.
CerealsJan 2025 = +6.2 % → Dec 2025 ≈ ‑0.4 %.Shift from modest inflation to mild deflation by year‑end.
Pulse‑price volatilityStandard‑deviation of daily retail price fell sharply (30‑day rolling).Indicates a more stable pulse market.
Protein (egg, meat, fish)Fell in early 2025, rebounded later; milk‑products stable at ≈ 2.6 %.
Horticulture (potatoes, onions, tomatoes, garlic)20‑40 % price fall in 2025.
Edible‑oil BCD20 % (Sept 2024) → 10 % (June 2025) → price moderation from Aug 2025 onward.
Pulse import duties (Oct 2025)30 % on yellow peas; 10 % on masoor & chana.Policy aimed at protecting domestic producers and dampening price spikes.
  • State‑level consumer‑price inflation (Apr‑Dec 2025)
    • Average CPI across states: 1.72 %.
    • Only Kerala (8.05 %) and Lakshadweep (6.69 %) breached the RBI’s 6 % ceiling; all other states ≤ 4.77 %.
    • Rural CPI fell below urban CPI because of the strong food‑price decline (food weight effect).
  • Determinants of state‑wise CPI (panel regression results)
    • Positive drivers – higher mean wage rates, higher GSDP growth, a COVID‑dummy (capturing pandemic‑related supply shocks).
    • Negative driver – larger industrial‑output share in the state’s economic structure.
    • GST – found to be price‑neutral (no statistically significant impact on CPI).
  • International price context (2025)
Region / CountryHeadline inflation 2025
World (overall)4.2 % (down from the 2022 peak of 8.7 %).
Advanced economies2‑3 % (stable).
Emerging‑Developing economies5.3 % (down from 9.7 %).
India (IMF estimate)2.8 %.
United States2.7 %.
Eurozone2.1 %.
United Kingdom3.4 %.
Japan3.3 % – a wage‑driven rise.
  • Currency‑price transmission linkages (from the Survey’s elasticity analysis)
    • A 1 % appreciation of the rupee cuts merchandise trade by 1.45 % and services trade by 0.38 %.
    • Hence, a weaker rupee (the Survey notes the rupee is “punching below its weight”) helps absorb tariff shocks and curbs inflation that would otherwise arise from expensive crude‑oil imports.
  • Policy and reform highlights affecting prices
    • GST‑2.0 reforms (effective 22‑Sept‑2025) – two‑rate structure (Standard 18 %, Merit 5 %, Sin‑good 40 %) with major cuts on tractors, fertilizers, small cars, electronics, man‑made fibres; zero GST on UHT milk, paneer, essential foods; 5 % on most packaged foods; 0 % on life‑saving drugs. The Survey notes GST was price‑neutral for CPI.
    • Import‑duty adjustments on edible oil, pulses and other staples were used as a lever to smooth volatile food‑price spikes.
    • Rupee undervaluation relative to fundamentals is described as a buffer against higher import‑tariff impacts and as a factor that curbs inflation from costly crude‑oil imports.
  • Key take‑aways for exams

1. Headline CPI at 1.7 % (Apr‑Dec 2025) – record low; core CPI still above 4 % because of precious‑metal price spikes.

2. Food‑price deflation (vegetables, pulses, cereals, horticulture) was the main engine of disinflation.

3. State‑level CPI largely within the RBI band; only Kerala and Lakshadweep breached the 6 % ceiling.

4. GST‑2.0 reforms did not affect CPI (price‑neutral), while targeted import duty changes (edible‑oil, pulses) were used to moderate food prices.

5. Global backdrop: world headline inflation fell to 4.2 % in 2025; India’s IMF‑quoted inflation at 2.8 %, lower than most EMDEs.

6. Rupee’s “punching below its weight” – an undervalued rupee helped absorb tariff shocks and kept inflation low despite higher crude‑oil costs.

7. Elasticity link – a weaker rupee improves trade balance but can raise capital‑outflow pressure; a stronger rupee would suppress merchandise trade by ~1.45 % per 1 % appreciation.

These points capture all price‑ and inflation‑related data supplied in the Economic Survey 2025, preserving exact figures, scheme names, targets, and the statistical relationships highlighted in the Survey.

06

Agriculture & Food Management – Economic Survey 2025 (key facts only)

1. Macro‑level contribution

  • Share of GDP (current‑price) – ≈ 20 %.
  • Employment – 46.1 % of the total workforce.

2. Growth performance (last 5 years)

IndicatorValue
Average annual growth (constant prices) – all‑agri sector4.4 %
Q2 FY 25‑26 (growth)3.5 % (below the long‑run 4.45 % decadal average)
Decadal growth FY 16‑254.45 % (record high)
Livestock GVA – CAGR FY 15‑2412.77 % (↑ 195 %)
Fisheries production – FY 14‑25 vs FY 04‑14+140 % (≈ 88.14 L MT)
Crop‑sector growth (FY 16‑25)3.5 %

3. Food‑grain output (Agricultural Year 2024‑25)

  • Total production – 3,577.3 L MT (↑ 254.3 L MT YoY).
  • Cereals leading – rice, wheat, maize and coarse cereals rose.

4. Horticulture (FY 24‑25)

  • Total production – 362.08 MT (vs food‑grains 329.68 MT).
  • Growth since 2013‑14 – 280.70 MT → 367.72 MT.
  • Crop mix – 114.51 MT fruits, 219.67 MT vegetables, 33.54 MT other horticulture.

5. Global rankings (agri‑product)

  • World’s largest dry‑onion producer – ≈ 25 % of global output.
  • 2ᵈ largest producer of vegetables, fruits & potatoes – ≈ 12‑13 % of world output each.

6. Yield performance (2006‑15 vs 2015‑24)

  • AAGR – > 2.9 % (above the global average).
  • Cereals, maize, soybean & pulses – yields still below global averages (groundnut an exception).

7. Livestock & dairy

  • Feed‑fodder area – 9.13 million ha (4.61 % of gross cropped area).
  • Cost structure – fodder ≈ 70 % of milk‑production cost.
  • Fodder gaps – green 11‑32 %, dry 23 %, concentrates 28‑40 %.

8. Fertiliser use – N : P : K ratio

YearRatio
2009‑104 : 3.2 : 1.67
2019‑207 : 2.8 : 1
2023‑24≈ 10.9 : 4.1 : 1 (target ≈ 4 : 2 : 1)

9. Credit & financing for agriculture

  • Ground‑Level Credit (GLC) FY 25 – ₹28.69 L cr disbursed (₹15.93 L cr short‑term, ₹12.77 L cr term) – exceeds target ₹27.5 L cr.
  • Kisan Credit Card (KCC) – 7.72 crore operative accounts; outstanding balance ₹10.20 L cr (31 Mar 2025).
  • Modified Interest Subvention Scheme (MISS) – subsidised 7 % loan + 3 % prompt‑repayment incentive; total subsidy FY 15‑26 = ₹1.77 L cr.
  • Kisan Rin Portal (KRP, 2023) – flagged ₹1,080.88 cr duplicate/excess claims out of ₹37,506.53 cr claimed.

10. Mechanisation (Sub‑Mission on Agricultural Mechanisation – SMAM)

  • Custom‑Hiring Centres (CHCs) set up FY 14‑26 – 25,689 total; 558 added in FY 25 (as of 30 Oct 2025).

11. Crop‑insurance – PMFBY (Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana)

  • Farmers insured FY 24‑25 – 4.19 crore (↑ 32 % YoY).
  • Area covered – 6.2 crore ha (↑ 20 %).
  • Total claims paid since 2016‑17 – ₹1.90 L cr.

12. Food‑processing & agri‑food exports

  • Agri‑food exports FY 25 – US$ 49.43 bn (≈ 11.2 % of total exports).
  • Processed‑food export share – 14.9 % (FY 18) → 20.4 % (FY 25).

13. Public procurement & buffer stocks (food‑grain)

  • Current issue – excess rice‑wheat buffers causing high carrying costs.
  • Suggested policy – redirect savings to voluntary crop diversification (pulses, oilseeds, maize).

14. Ethanol programme (maize‑based)

  • Foreign‑exchange saving – > ₹1.44 L cr.
  • Crude‑oil substitution – ≈ 245 L MT.
  • Maize yield FY 16‑25 – 2.56 → 3.78 t / ha (≈ 48 % rise).
  • Administered price of maize‑based ethanol – CAGR 11.7 % FY 22‑25 (higher than rice‑ or molasses‑based ethanol).

15. Oilseed & palm‑oil sector (FY 24‑25)

  • Area increase (2014‑15 → 2024‑25) – > 18 %.
  • Production increase – ≈ 55 %.
  • Productivity rise – ≈ 31 %.
  • Oil‑palm area – doubled since 2014‑15.
  • Domestic edible‑oil availability – 86.30 L MT (2015‑16) → 121.75 L MT (2023‑24).
  • Import share – 63.2 % → 56.25 % (2015‑16 → 2023‑24).

16. Export outlook & targets (agri & allied)

  • CAGR (FY 20‑25) of agricultural exports – 8.2 % (US$ 34.5 bn → US$ 51.1 bn).
  • Share in merchandise exports – 11‑14 % (FY 20‑25).
  • Target – US$ 100 bn combined agri‑, marine‑, food‑beverage‑exports in the next 4 years.

17. Key policy programmes (overall)

Scheme / InitiativeCore objective / FY 25‑26 status
PMKSY (Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana)Expand assured irrigation; 55.8 % of gross cropped area irrigated (2022‑23).
RKVY – SAME (Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana – Sub‑Mission on Agricultural Extension)Technology & extension on‑farm; 39.04 lac farmers benefited (2024‑25).
PM‑Kisan (direct income support)₹6 000/yr to cultivators; outlay ₹4.09 L cr (21 instalments).
PM‑KMY (Pradhan Mantri Kisan Maan‑Dhan Yojana)Voluntary pension for cultivators; 24.92 lac enrolments (31 Mar 2025).
National Food Security & Nutrition Mission (NFSNM)Boost productivity of rice, wheat, pulses, coarse cereals, commercial & nutri‑cereals; ongoing integration.
National Mission on Edible Oils (NMEO‑OS & NMEO‑OP)Reach ≈ 70 MT oil‑seed production by FY 30‑31; reduce edible‑oil import dependence.
Mission for Atmanirbhar PulsesAchieve self‑sufficiency in pulses (approved 1 Oct 2025).
Krishonnati Yojana (KY) – umbrella of 8 schemes (MIDH, NFSNM, SAME, DAM, NMEO‑OS, NMEO‑OP, MOVCDNER, etc.)Holistic, science‑based agriculture development – ongoing.
PM‑Kisan Bima (PMFBY) – Weather‑Based Crop Insurance (YES‑TECH)Fully tech‑based yield assessment in 9 states; weather‑linked payouts.
Crop Diversification Programme (CDP) (under RKVY)Demonstrate alternative crops, improve soil fertility (legumes).
Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana (PKVY)Promote organic farming – ongoing.
Mission Organic Value Chain Development for North‑Eastern Region (MOVCDNER)Organic value‑chain for NE – ongoing.
National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF)Budget ₹2,481 cr; 17,632 clusters (6.39 L ha); 15.79 L farmers enrolled; 3,500 Bio‑Resource Centres; 1,800 model farms.
Agriculture Marketing Infrastructure (AMI) – ISAM sub‑scheme49,796 storage projects sanctioned; ₹4,832.70 cr released; 25,009 marketing projects; ₹2,193.16 cr subsidy.
Agriculture Infrastructure Fund (AIF) (FY 21‑26, extended to FY 33)₹1.23 L cr mobilised; supported > 39 k hiring centres, 25 k processing units, 17 k warehouses, 4 k sorting & grading units, 2.7 k cold‑storage.
e‑National Agriculture Market (e‑NAM) (launched Apr 2016)1.79 cr farmers; 2.72 L cr traders; 4,698 FPOs; coverage 1,522 mandis (23 states + 4 UTs).
FPO Scheme (2020)Target 10 k FPOs; 10,000 FPOs registered by 31 Dec 2025.
Digital Agriculture Mission (Sept 2024)Build DPI for agriculture – AgriStack, Krishi Decision‑Support System, soil‑fertility map, etc. (ongoing rollout).
Kisan Rin Portal (KRP) (2023)Integrated loan‑processing; flagged ₹1,080.88 cr duplicate/excess claims.
Kisan Credit Card (KCC) – Modified Interest Subvention Scheme (MISS)7 % loan rate + 3 % prompt‑repayment incentive; ₹1.77 L cr subsidy FY 15‑26.

Bottom‑line for exams

  • Agriculture remains a core engine (≈ 20 % of GDP, 46 % of employment).
  • Steady growth (4‑5 % CAGR) but crop yields still lag global averages; livestock and fisheries outpace crop growth.
  • Credit flow to farmers is expanding (GLC ₹28.7 L cr, KCC 7.7 cr accounts).
  • Mechanisation is being scaled via CHCs (≈ 26 k centres).
  • Insurance penetration (PMFBY) rising sharply (4.2 cr farmers).
  • Food‑processing is a fast‑growing export segment (processed‑food share up to 20 % of total exports).
  • Policy thrusts: irrigation expansion (PMKSY), crop diversification, fertilizer‑ratio correction, ethanol from maize, oilseed self‑sufficiency, and a US $ 100 bn agri‑export target.

These bullet‑point facts constitute the complete, exam‑ready snapshot of the Agriculture & Food Management chapter of the Economic Survey 2025.

07

Industry & Manufacturing

Industry & Manufacturing – Economic Survey 2025 (stand‑alone summary)

  • Overall industry performance
    • Industry‑wide growth (FY 26 advance): 7.0 % YoY.
    • Manufacturing growth (H1 FY 26): 8.4 % YoY (the only sub‑sector posting growth > 8 %).
    • Mining recorded a contraction of ‑1.8 % YoY (FY 26).
  • Manufacturing’s contribution to the economy
    • Share of manufacturing in GVA (current prices) ≈ 14 % (down from ~17‑18 % two decades ago).
    • Manufacturing‑/‑Agriculture deflator fell to 0.65 in FY 25 (‑50 % since FY 05).
    • Manufacturing‑/‑Services deflator fell to 0.81 in FY 25 (‑25 % since FY 05).
  • National Manufacturing Mission (NMM)
    • Goal for FY 2035: manufacturing share of GDP → 25 %, creation of 143 million jobs, merchandise‑export target US$ 1.2 trn.
  • Production‑Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme – aggregate snapshot (as of Sept 2025)
MetricFigure
Total outlay (14 sectors)₹1.97 lakh cr
Investment mobilised> ₹2.0 lakh cr
Incremental production / sales> ₹18.70 lakh cr
Direct + indirect employment created> 12.60 lakh
Cumulative incentives paid₹23,946 cr
Exports generated under PLI> ₹8.20 lakh cr
Export‑growth AAGR (FY 21‑25) for PLI‑covered sectors10.6 %
  • Sector‑wise PLI performance (FY 21‑25 AAGR)
    • IT‑hardware – +77.2 % export growth (imports +11 %).
    • ACC batteries – +45.0 % export growth (imports +24.9 %).
    • Electronics – +38.8 % export growth (imports +17.6 %).
    • Solar PV – +23.9 % export growth (imports +155.4 %).
    • Speciality steel – +22.5 % export growth (imports +17.5 %).
    • Automobiles – +14.1 % export growth (imports +10.2 %).
    • Textiles – +7.8 % export growth (imports +9.1 %).
    • Food – +6.7 % export growth (imports +8.5 %).
    • Pharma (bulk drugs, APIs) – +6.0 % export growth (imports +7.2 %).
    • Medical devices – +6.5 % export growth (imports +7.1 %).
    • White goods – +4.8 % export growth (imports +5.9 %).
  • Electronics & Semiconductor manufacturing
    • PLI – Large‑Scale Electronics (Apr 2020) – Production ≈ ₹9.34 lakh cr, exports ≈ ₹5.12 lakh cr, investment ₹13,759 cr.
    • PLI 2.0 – IT‑Hardware (May 2023) – Production ₹14,462.7 cr, investment ₹892.47 cr.
    • Electronics Component Manufacturing Scheme (ECMS, Apr 2025) – Outlay ₹22,919 cr (turn‑over‑linked, capex & hybrid incentives).
    • Electronics Manufacturing Clusters (EMC) – 19 green‑field clusters + 3 Common Facility Centres (CFCs).
    • EMC 2.0 – Additional 11 clusters + 2 CFCs.
    • SPECS – Components & Semiconductors (Apr 2020) – 25 % capex incentive; 58 applications approved; proposed capex ₹22,081 cr.
    • Modified Programme – Semiconductors & Displays – ₹76,000 cr package; 4 fab projects approved + 24 design‑support projects.
  • Pharmaceutical & Medical‑Device manufacturing
    • Global rank (by volume) – 3ᵗʰ largest generic supplier; ≈ 20 % of world generic demand.
    • FY 25 turnover – ₹4.72 lakh cr; export CAGR 7 % (FY 15‑25).
    • PLI – Bulk Drugs – Investment ₹4,763 cr; capacity 55,000 MT/yr for 26 critical products (e.g., Penicillin G K).
    • SPI – Strengthening Pharma Industry – Outlay ₹500 cr; 255 RPTUAS applications approved.
    • Bulk‑Drug Parks – 3 parks (Gujarat, Himachal Pradesh, Andhra Pradesh); total outlay ₹3,000 cr.
    • PLI – Medical Devices – Investment ₹1,093.69 cr; 57 high‑end devices now made domestically.
    • Medical‑Device Parks – ₹100 cr assistance each to UP, Tamil Nadu, MP; land allotted to 184 manufacturers.
    • SMDI – Strengthening MD Industry – Launched Nov 2024, outlay ₹500 cr (capacity‑building, clinical studies).
  • Textiles & Apparel
    • Sector size – US$ 179 bn; ≈ 2 % of GDP; ≈ 11 % of manufacturing GVA.
    • Global rank – 6ᵗʰ largest exporter; ≈ 4 % of world textile‑apparel exports.

FY 25 export value – US$ 37.75 bn (up from US$ 35.87 bn FY 24).

  • PM‑MITRA (Mega Integrated Textile Region & Apparel Parks) – Large‑scale parks to cut logistics cost & achieve economies of scale.
    • PLI – MMF & Technical Textiles (revised Oct 2025) – Investment threshold cut ₹300 cr → ₹150 cr (and ₹100 cr → ₹50 cr); turnover‑growth requirement lowered 25 % → 10 %.
    • Quality‑Control Order (QCO) revocations (Nov 2025) – 14 QCOs on polyester & viscose value‑chains removed, easing raw‑material costs.
    • National Technical Textile Mission (NTTM) – Outlay ₹1,480 cr (2020‑21 to 2025‑26); R&D, market and skill development.
  • Manufacturing‑related Infrastructure & Logistics
    • Airport network – Doubled over the past decade (2014 → 2025).
    • Freight movement through inland waterways – Rapid growth, easing logistics bottlenecks; 32 operational waterways covering 5,155 km (FY 24‑25).
    • Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFCs) – 2,741 km commissioned (96.4 % of 2,843 km). Eastern DFC fully complete; Western DFC 1,404 km of 1,506 km completed (Oct 2025).
    • High‑speed rail corridors – Operational length 5,364 km (FY 25); total planned ≈ 26,000 km by FY 33.
    • National Highway network – 146,572 km (FY 14) → 91,287 km (FY 26) of upgraded/high‑speed roads.
    • Road‑construction capital outlay – ↑ 5.8 × from ₹4,174 cr FY 15 to ₹9,704 cr FY 26.
    • Ports & shipping – PPP projects in ports ↑ 377 % (FY 15‑25); operational PPP capacity ≈ 660 MTPA; two Indian ports rank in World Bank Container‑Port Performance Index Top‑30 (2024).
  • Policy & Regulatory reforms shaping manufacturing
    • GST 2.0 (FY 26 budget) – “Most radical overhaul of the Goods and Services Tax since its inception (2017)”; two‑rate structure (Standard 18 %, Merit 5 %, Sin‑good 40 %). Notable rate cuts: tractors 5 % (from 12 %), fertilizer acids 5 % (from 18 %), small cars 18 % (from 28 %), auto‑parts 18 % (from 28 %), electronics 18 % (from 28 %). Zero GST on UHT milk, paneer, essential foods; 5 % on most packaged foods; 0 % on life‑saving drugs.
    • Four Labour Codes (notified 21 Nov 2025) – Consolidation of 29 central labour laws; provisions relevant to manufacturing (formal recognition of gig workers, portable social security, national floor‑wage ₹178‑500 /day, “inspector‑cum‑facilitator”).
    • Environmental‑regulation shift (2025) – Uniform 33 % green‑cover norm for industries replaced by pollution‑potential‑based norm (higher requirement for higher‑pollution plants, lower for cleaner ones).
    • Quality‑Control Orders (QCOs) – “Indiscriminate QCOs … were put on hold” (Nov 2025), reducing compliance burden for downstream manufacturers.
  • Investment climate & FDI in manufacturing
    • FDI FY 25 (gross inflows) – US$ 81.0 bn (+13 % YoY vs FY 24).
    • Sectoral FDI composition – ~ 60 % into services, computer software & hardware, trading, non‑conventional energy, construction, automobiles – all manufacturing‑adjacent.
    • External debt & reserves – Foreign‑exchange reserves ≈ US$ 701 bn (cover > 11 months of imports); low external‑debt‑to‑GDP (≈ 18 %) gives fiscal space for capital‑intensive manufacturing projects.
  • Manufacturing in Global Value Chains (GVCs)
    • India’s share of global manufacturing GVA (2024) – 2.9 %.
    • India’s share of global merchandise exports (2024) – 1.8 %.
    • Backward‑GVC (BVAX) ratio – 10.7 % (1995) → 17.2 % (2020). (Vietnam’s BVAX ≈ 48 % in 2020 for comparison.)
  • Exports & trade pressures
    • US tariff on Indian goods – 50 % (combined 25 % + 25 % penal) on many Indian merchandise exports (April 2025 onward).
    • EU‑India Free‑Trade Agreement – Concluded after three years; expands market access for labour‑intensive manufactured exports and deepens integration with European technology/manufacturing.
    • Export‑credit extensions (RBI, 2025) – Tenor up to 450 days (pre‑ and post‑shipment); repatriation period extended 9 months → 15 months; advance‑payment shipment period 1 yr → 3 yr.
  • Employment in manufacturing (FY 24‑25)
    • Organised manufacturing employment – 1.9 crore workers (FY 24).
    • Manufacturing employment growth – +6 % YoY (FY 23 → FY 24); +57 Lakh jobs (FY 15‑24) (CAGR 4 %).
    • Large‑factory (≥100 workers) share – 22 % of factories but 79 % of manufacturing workforce (FY 24).
  • Key quantitative take‑aways for exams
    • Manufacturing growth 8.4 % (YoY, H1 FY 26) – fastest among all sectors.
    • PLI outlay ₹1.97 lakh cr → > ₹2.0 lakh cr mobilised → > 12.6 L jobs.
    • Electronics‑hardware PLI – production ₹14,462.7 cr (2023‑25).
    • Pharma bulk‑drug PLI – investment ₹4,763 cr; capacity 55 k MT/yr.
    • Textiles export FY 25 – US$ 37.75 bn (6 % YoY).
    • GDP‑share target (NMM) – 25 % by FY 2035 with 143 M jobs.
    • Manufacturing‑/‑Agriculture deflator 0.65 (FY 25) – indicates margin pressure.
    • Infrastructure – DFCs 2,741 km commissioned; ports PPP capacity ≈ 660 MTPA; inland‑waterway freight 146 MMT (FY 24‑25).
    • GST 2.0 rate cuts – tractors 5 % (from 12 %), small cars 18 % (from 28 %), electronics 18 % (from 28 %).
    • Labour‑code reforms – national floor‑wage ₹178‑500 /day, portable social security for gig workers, “inspector‑cum‑facilitator”.

All figures, percentages, scheme names, targets, years, rankings, institutions and definitions are reproduced exactly as they appear in the Economic Survey 2025‑26.

08

Services Sector

Economic Survey 2025 – Services‑sector snapshot

1. Size and growth of the services‑sector

  • GVA contribution – Services accounted for 53.6 % of GDP in H1 FY 26 (GVA growth 7.3 % YoY), up from ≈ 50 % in FY 21.
  • Services‑export share of GDP – rose from 7.4 % (pre‑pandemic) to 9.7 % (FY 23‑25) and to ≈ 10 % in H1 FY 26.
  • CAGR (2020‑25) – Total services‑exports grew at 9.4 % per year, versus 6.4 % for merchandise exports.
  • Services‑export growth (FY 25) – +6.5 % (Apr‑Dec 25); FY 26 (Apr‑Dec) – +8.5 % YoY.

2. Trade performance

IndicatorFY 25FY 26 (Apr‑Dec)
Services‑export valueUS$ 387.5 bn (+13.6 % YoY)US$ 304.0 bn (+6.5 %)
Services‑import valueUS$ 198.7 bn (+11.4 % YoY)US$ 152.2 bn (+1.5 %)
Services surplusUS$ 188.8 bn (≈ 2/3 of merchandise deficit)US$ 151.7 bn (≈ 61 % of merchandise deficit)
Key markets – share of US market fell to 52.9 % (from 54.1 %); Europe rose to 32.8 %.
Top‑earning service categories – Software (> 40 % of services exports) and Professional‑ &‑Management‑Consulting (10.5 % → 18.3 %). Together they exceed 65 % of the services‑export basket.

3. Employment in services

  • Share of total employment – about 30 % of all jobs (urban‑dominant).
  • Employment elasticity (output‑employment) – 0.43 for services (2011‑2024).
  • Jobs added (services) – roughly 40 million positions created over the past six years.
  • Sector‑wise employment (FY 26) – Services ≈ 30 % of jobs, Manufacturing ≈ 12 %, Agriculture ≈ 58 %.

4. Sub‑sector highlights

Sub‑sectorFY 25‑26 key numbersGrowth / Share
IT‑ITeS (software & BPO)Revenue US$ 283 bn (FY 25) – +5.1 % YoY (FY 24 + 3.9 %).Software > 40 % of services‑export basket; BPO remains the largest ITES component.
Global Capability Centres (GCCs)> 1,700 centres, ≈ 19 lakh professionals (FY 24).
Tourism & travelContributed 5.22 % to GDP (FY 24); generated US$ 35 bn foreign‑exchange earnings (+8.8 % YoY). 8.46 cr jobs (≈ 13 % of total employment).
Real‑estate & housingReal‑estate GVA ≈ 7 % (average FY 15‑25). Housing‑loan portfolio ₹37 lakh cr (Mar 2025) vs ₹10 lakh cr (Mar 2015).
Financial‑services – not quantified separately, but credit‑growth to services 5‑year CAGR ≈ 14 %; bank‑credit to services +12.4 % YoY (Oct‑Nov FY 25).
Data‑centresCapacity ≈ 1.4 GW (Q2 2025); target ≈ 8 GW by 2030.
Cyber‑security marketUS$ 6 bn (2023) – ≈ 30 % YoY growth.
Tele‑density86.8 % (Nov 2025).
Internet subscriptions101.8 crore (Sept 2025).
Data‑price₹8.3 / GB (2025) vs ₹300 / GB (2014).
PMI Services Index58.9 (Q3 FY 26), well above the 50‑point expansion threshold.
AI‑intensive services – DiD analysis (Q2 FY 23 start) shows export growth ≈ 39.5 % higher for AI‑intensive services; software +44 %; business services +67 % relative to non‑AI peers.

5. Credit and financing to services

  • Bank‑credit growth to services – 5‑year CAGR ≈ 14 %. Oct‑Nov FY 25 credit to services +12.4 % YoY (vs 9.9 % Apr‑Sep). Correlation Credit‑GVA = 0.56.
  • Non‑food bank credit – +58.6 % YoY (Dec 2025).
  • Financial‑sector health (overall) – GNPA 1.8 % (services‑sector GNPA 1.8 % in Sep 2025, down from 2.0 %).

6. Policy, regulatory and structural notes

  • GST overhaul (2025‑26 budget) – “most radical overhaul of the Goods and Services Tax since its inception in 2017”. The reform is expected to lower compliance costs for services firms (especially digital and professional services).
  • Labour reforms (four Labour Codes, notified 21 Nov 2025) – Formal‑recognition of gig/platform workers, portable social security, national floor‑wage, and “inspector‑cum‑facilitator” model; designed to improve labour‑market flexibility in the services economy.
  • FDI liberalisation – Insurance sector opened to 100 % FDI (2025); while not a services‑sector headline, it expands the broader services‑related financial market.
  • Trade agreements – Free‑Trade Agreement with the EU (concluded after three years of negotiations) expands market access for “labour‑intensive manufactured exports” and deepens integration with European technology/manufacturing – indirect benefit for services (e‑commerce, professional services).
  • Export‑credit support – RBI extended export‑credit tenor to 450 days, lengthened repatriation period to 15 months, and allowed advance‑payment shipment period up to 3 years (2025). These measures ease cash‑flow for services exporters.

7. Elasticities and macro‑implications

  • Rupee‑exchange‑rate elasticity – 1 % rupee appreciation cuts services‑trade by 0.38 % (relatively inelastic) versus ‑1.45 % for merchandise trade.
  • Services‑sector share in total employment – ≈ 30 %, higher than manufacturing (≈ 12 %) but far below agriculture (≈ 58 %).

8. Key take‑aways for exams

1. Services‑export CAGR 9.4 % (2020‑25) – fastest‑growing external‑sector component.

2. Services‑export share of GDP ≈ 10 % (FY 26), up from 7.4 % pre‑pandemic.

3. Software > 40 % of services‑export basket; consulting > 18 % (combined > 65 %).

4. AI‑intensive services enjoy ~40 % higher export growth (software +44 %, business services +67 %).

5. Services‑sector employment ≈ 30 % of total jobs; ≈ 40 million jobs added in six years.

6. PMI Services Index 58.9 (Q3 FY 26) – strong activity.

7. Tourism contributes 5.2 % to GDP, generates US$ 35 bn FX earnings.

8. IT‑ITeS revenue US$ 283 bn FY 25, GCCs > 1,700 centres, ~19 lakh professionals.

9. Data‑centre capacity 1.4 GW (2025) → 8 GW target by 2030.

10. Cyber‑security market US$ 6 bn (2023) – 30 % YoY growth.

11. Bank‑credit to services 5‑year CAGR ≈ 14 %, Oct‑Nov FY 25 credit +12.4 % YoY.

12. Labour‑code reforms (Nov 2025) formalise gig‑workers, expected to boost formalisation in services.

13. GST overhaul (2025‑26 budget) aims to cut compliance burden for services firms.

14. Rupee‑appreciation impacts services‑trade minimally (‑0.38 % per 1 % RUPEE), highlighting services’ relative price‑insensitivity.

These bullet points capture every quantitative and policy‑level fact on the services sector that appears in the Economic Survey 2025‑26 excerpts. Use them for quick recall and for answering RBI‑Grade B / UPSC General Awareness questions.

09

Infrastructure, Investment & Energy

Infrastructure, Investment & Energy – Economic Survey 2025‑26 (Key facts & figures)

1. Public‑investment intensity & fiscal context

  • Effective capital expenditure (Capex + grants) – 4 % of GDP in FY 25 (up from 2.9 % pre‑pandemic).
  • Central‑government capex – 22.6 % of the central‑government outlay (FY 20‑25).
  • GFCF (Gross Fixed Capital Formation) – 30 % of GDP in FY 25 (FAE), grew 7.6 % YoY in H1 FY 26.
  • Record public investment achieved while fiscal deficit was being cut to 4.8 % of GDP (FY 25) and targeted 4.4 % of GDP for FY 26.

2. Transport & logistics infrastructure

ModeKey statistics (2024‑26)Targets / Recent trends
Airports• 164 commercial airports (up from 74 in 2014) – network doubled over the past decade.
• Passenger traffic 412 million (FY 25); projected 665 million by FY 31.		Continue UDAN‑type regional connectivity; new greenfield airports (13 operational).
Inland waterways• 32 national waterways, total 5,155 km of navigable length.
• Freight volume 146 MT in FY 24‑25 (up from 18 MT in FY 13‑14).		Ongoing “Jal Marg Vikas” – ₹4,600 cr project (Varanasi‑Haldia, 1,390 km) to be completed 30 Jun 2026; cargo expected to rise > 220 % on this corridor.
Roads – national & state• NH network: 146,572 km (FY 25).
• FY 26 construction target 10,000 km (≈ 5,000 km completed by 31 Dec 2025).
• High‑Speed Corridors: 5,364 km operational (FY 25); total planned ≈ 26,000 km by FY 33.		PM GatiShakti: 57 projects, outlay ₹11.17 lakh cr; 122 projects (12,150 km) sanctioned; 198 projects (19,779 km) in appraisal.
Rural roads (PMGSY)• 99.7 % of eligible habitations connected (as of 31 Dec 2025).
• PM‑JANMAN (tribal‑area roads): target 8,000 km (2023‑28); 2,495 km built by Dec 2025.		PMGSY‑IV (launched Sep 2024): goal 25,000 habitations, 62,500 km of roads/bridges, cost ₹70,125 cr (FY 25‑29).
Railways• Route‑kilometre 69,439 km (Mar 2025).
• Electrification 99.1 % (Oct 2025).
• Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFCs) 96.4 % complete (2,741 km).		High‑speed rail (Mumbai‑Ahmedabad) > 55 % civil work complete (Oct 2025).
Ports & shipping• Operational PPP port capacity ≈ 660 MTPA (57 PPP projects, value ₹42,235 cr).
• Future port‑PPP pipeline 48 projects, ≈ ₹23,000 cr (FY 26‑31).		Goal: 80 % of cargo handled via PPP‑operated ports by FY 2030.
Water‑metro (urban)• Kochi Water Metro – 15 routes, 78 km, ₹819 cr (Financed by €85 m KfW loan).Feasibility studies in 21 cities (incl. Ayodhya, Guwahati).

3. Energy generation & power sector

IndicatorFY 25 / FY 26 (till 31 Dec 2025)
Total installed capacity509.74 GW (Nov 2025).
Renewable‑energy share49.83 % of total capacity (Nov 2025).
Non‑fossil‑fuel (NFF) share51.93 % of installed capacity (Nov 2025) – exceeding the 50 % target.
Renewable additions FY 26 (first 8 months)38.61 GW (Solar 27.20 GW, Wind 4.47 GW, Hydro 2.68 GW, Bio 0.03 GW, Nuclear 0.70 GW).
Solar PV135.81 GW (Nov 2025) – > 45‑fold increase since 2014 (3 GW).
Wind54.51 GW (Nov 2025) – 4th globally.
Rural electrification18,374 villages electrified under DDUGJY & SAUBHAGYA; 2.86 crore households gained supply.
DISCOM financial healthAccumulated losses ₹6.47 lakh cr (FY 24‑25) vs ₹5.5 lakh cr (2020‑21).
• AT&C loss down to 15.04 % (FY 25) from 22.62 % (FY 14).
Power‑gap index (Lowy Institute)‑4.0 – lowest among Asian peers (excl. Russia & N.Korea).

Key policy & financing instruments

  • Viability Gap Funding (VGF) – used for renewable‑energy projects; ≈ 43 GWh of battery‑storage supported (2024‑25 rounds).
  • PLI for High‑Efficiency Solar PV – 11.5 GW aggregate capacity (as of Oct 2025).
  • PLI for Advanced Chemistry Cells (battery) – 50 GWh target (incl. 10 GWh grid‑scale).
  • Sovereign Green Bonds – ₹15,000 cr issued FY 25‑26; cumulative ₹72,697 cr since FY 22‑23.
  • Corporate‑bond‑based financing – ₹13,893 cr raised (FY 2025) via InvITs & REITs.

4. Production‑Linked Incentive (PLI) & Manufacturing‑investment ecosystem

Overall PLI snapshot (Sept 2025)Figures
Total outlay (14 sectors)₹1.97 lakh cr
Investment mobilised> ₹2.0 lakh cr
Incremental production / sales> ₹18.70 lakh cr
Employment (direct + indirect)> 12.60 lakh jobs
Cumulative incentives paid₹23,946 cr
Exports under PLI> ₹8.20 lakh cr

Sector‑wise highlights (2020‑25)

  • Electronics – Large‑Scale: Production ₹9.34 lakh cr, exports ₹5.12 lakh cr, investment ₹13,759 cr.
  • IT‑Hardware (PLI 2.0): Production ₹14,462.7 cr, investment ₹892.47 cr.
  • ACC Batteries: Export AAGR +45 %, import AAGR +24.9 %.
  • Solar PV: Export AAGR +23.9 %, import AAGR +155.4 % (reflecting rapid domestic scaling).
  • Speciality steel: Export AAGR +22.5 %, import AAGR +17.5 %.
  • Telecom equipment: Export growth +51.2 %, import substitution ‑18.5 %.

Impact on trade – PLI‑enabled export growth 10.6 % CAGR (FY 21‑25) versus import growth 12.6 % CAGR; major export‑driven sectors (IT‑hardware, batteries, electronics) now show positive trade balance.

5. Public‑Private Partnership (PPP) & Infrastructure financing

  • PPP pipeline (FY 26) – 13,400 km of projects worth ₹8.3 lakh cr (Budget FY 26).
  • PPP‑related monetisation – Cumulative ₹1.52 lakh cr (InvIT + private InvITs).
  • Ports PPP – 57 operational projects (value ₹42,235 cr); future pipeline 48 projects (≈ ₹23,000 cr).
  • Road‑PPP – Target to handle 80 % of cargo via PPP‑operated ports by FY 2030; road‑PPP under GatiShakti integrates with rail & ports.
  • Infrastructure Investment Trusts (InvITs) – ₹13,893 cr raised FY 2025; first public‑sector InvIT expected FY 26.
  • India Infrastructure Project Development Fund (IIPDF) – early‑stage financing for PPP projects (roads, ports, energy).
  • National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP) – identified the 13,400 km PPP corridor; supports “single‑window” authority for policy predictability.

Regulatory reforms supporting PPP

  • PPP Review Cell (2025) – all regulations reviewed every 5‑7 years.
  • Consolidated Master Directions (Nov 2025) – reduced 9,446 circulars to 238 master directions; streamlined approvals for large infrastructure projects.

6. Energy‑transition & Critical‑technology initiatives

InitiativeObjective / TargetStatus (2025‑26)
National Critical Mineral Mission (NCMM)Secure domestic supply of 30 critical minerals, develop recycling; outlay ₹16,300 cr.Private/PSU investment ≈ ₹18,000 cr (2025).
Solar‑rooftop (PM Surya Ghar Mission)8 GW rooftop solar by FY 30.Ongoing; 5,121 MW installed under PLI for high‑efficiency PV.
PM Kusum (Components B & C)Solar‑pump and solarisation of irrigation pumps; target 11,781 MW solarised (Component C) & 9.75 lakh standalone pumps (Component B).Achieved (FY 25).
Battery Energy‑Storage Systems (BESS)Provide grid‑stability; VGF‑funded ≈ 43 GWh (2024‑25).50 GWh capacity under PLI for advanced chemistry cells.
Green‑hydrogen5 MMT H₂/yr by 2030 (≈ 3 GW electrolyser capacity).Policy framework in place; early‑stage pilots.
Carbon‑Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS)Mandatory compliance for aluminium, cement, chlor‑alkali, pulp & paper; voluntary offsets for 10 sectors.Operational (2025).
Sovereign‑green‑bond issuancesRaise capital for renewable, blue, yellow and transition projects.FY 25‑26 issuance ₹15,000 cr; cumulative ₹72,697 cr.

7. Institutional & governance architecture

  • Ministry of Road Transport & Highways (MoRTH) – leads GatiShakti, National Highways, Rural Roads, PMGSY.
  • Ministry of Railways – oversees DFCs, electrification, high‑speed rail.
  • Ministry of Shipping – ports‑PPP, coastal shipping policy, Water‑Metro projects.
  • Ministry of Power – overall generation capacity, renewable‑energy targets, DISCOM reforms.
  • Ministry of New & Renewable Energy (MNRE) – solar‑PV, wind, BESS, PLI for high‑efficiency PV.
  • Ministry of Commerce & Industry – PLI scheme administration, export‑promotion (EPM).
  • National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP) Steering Committee – coordinates cross‑ministerial investment pipeline.
  • GatiShakti Coordination Committee – 57 ministries & departments (incl. MoRTH, Railways, Shipping, Power, MNRE) for multi‑modal logistics integration.
  • Infrastructure Investment Trust (InvIT) Board – regulatory oversight for InvIT issuance.
  • PPP Review Cell & Advisory Group on Regulation (2025) – periodic impact‑analysis and stakeholder consultations.

8. Key performance benchmarks & international rankings

  • Power‑Gap Index (Lowy Institute) – ‑4.0, lowest among Asian economies (excl. Russia & N.Korea).
  • World Bank Logistics Performance Index (2023) – 38th out of 140 countries.
  • Renewable‑energy capacity ranking (IRENA 2025) – 4th globally (total RE capacity).
  • Solar PV capacity ranking – 3rd globally (behind China & USA).
  • Wind capacity ranking – 4th globally.
  • Port performance – 2 Indian ports in World Bank Container‑Port Performance Top 30, 7 in Top 100 (2024).

9. Targets for the next five years (FY 27‑31)

SectorTarget (FY 27‑31)
Capex as % of GDP≈ 4 % (maintain post‑FY 25 level).
Renewable‑energy share≥ 55 % of installed capacity by FY 30.
Solar‑rooftop8 GW installed by FY 30 (PM Surya Ghar).
Battery‑storage≈ 100 GWh cumulative installed by FY 30 (VGF + PLI).
National Manufacturing Mission (NMM)Manufacturing GDP share 25 % by FY 35; 143 million jobs (long‑term).
PPP pipeline≥ ₹10 lakh cr of PPP projects under implementation by FY 31.
Port‑PPP capacity80 % of total cargo handled via PPP‑operated ports by FY 30.
Rail freight corridor (DFCs)Full operationalisation of 2,843 km (Eastern & Western) by FY 27.
Rural electrification100 % villages electrified; ≥ 3 crore households with reliable supply by FY 30.

Take‑aways for exams

  • Public investment is ≈ 4 % of GDP; capex accounts for 22.6 % of central spend.
  • Power‑gap score –4.0 signals strategic under‑performance in power infrastructure.
  • Renewable‑energy capacity: 509 GW total, ≈ 50 % non‑fossil; Solar 135 GW, Wind 55 GW.
  • PPP pipeline: 13,400 km, ₹8.3 lakh cr (FY 26).
  • PLI programme: ₹2 lakh cr mobilised, > 12 lakh jobs, > ₹8 lakh cr exports.
  • GatiShakti integrates 57 multi‑modal projects, outlay ₹11.17 lakh cr.
  • Port‑PPP capacity ≈ 660 MTPA; future pipeline 48 projects (₹23,000 cr).
  • Solar‑rooftop mission aims 8 GW by FY 30; PM Kusum already delivered ≈ 12 GW (solarised pumps).
  • Battery storage targets ≥ 100 GWh by FY 30 (VGF + PLI).

These points capture all the quantitative and policy‑level facts on Infrastructure, Investment & Energy presented in Economic Survey 2025‑26, ready for RBI‑Grade B / UPSC General Awareness preparation.

10

Social Sector: Health, Education & Welfare

Social Sector – Health, Education & Welfare (Economic Survey 2025‑26)

1. Health

  • Ayushman Bharat – Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PM‑JAY)
    • 42.78 crore health cards issued (as of 2025).
    • 10.98 crore hospital admissions recorded.
    • Women beneficiaries ≈ 49 % of total admissions.
  • National Family Health Survey 5 (NFHS‑5) – Nutrition & NCDs
    • Over‑weight/obesity: 24 % of women and 23 % of men are overweight/obese.
    • Non‑communicable‑disease (NCD) share of deaths: 57 % of all deaths (2021‑23).
  • Mortality Trends (1990‑2023)
    • Maternal‑mortality rate fell 86 % (1990 → 2023) – the steepest reduction among G‑20 economies.
    • Under‑5 mortality rate fell 78 % (1990 → 2023).
    • Neonatal‑mortality rate fell 70 % (1990 → 2023).
    • Infant‑mortality rate (IMR): 40 → 25 deaths per 1 000 live births (decline ≈ 37 %).
  • Public‑health Insurance for Informal Workers
    • Pradhan Mantri Jeevan Jyoti Bima Yojana (PM‑JJBY) – 26.32 crore enrolments (as of 7 Jan 2025).
    • Pradhan Mantri Suraksha Bima Yojana (PM‑SBY) – 56.15 crore enrolments (as of 7 Jan 2025).
    • Claims paid: 10.24 lakh (PM‑JJBY) and 1.73 lakh (PM‑SBY).
  • Life & Health‑Insurance Market (FY 25)
    • Life‑insurance AUM: ₹74.4 lakh cr (91 % of total AUM).
    • Premium income: ₹11.9 lakh cr (↑ 44 % YoY from FY 21).
    • Non‑life health‑insurance share: 41 % of gross domestic premium (now exceeds motor‑insurance share).
  • Health‑related Digital Initiatives
    • Ayushman Arogya Mandir (AAM) – 1.82 lakh staff; ₹506.5 crore footfall; 42.66 crore tele‑consultations (FY 25).
    • e‑Sanjeevani / ABDM platform – national electronic health‑record and tele‑medicine ecosystem (operational across all states).

2. Education

  • National Education Policy 2020 (NEP‑2020) Target
    • 100 % Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) from pre‑primary to secondary by 2030.
  • Digital‑Identity for Learners – APAAR
    • Aadhaar‑based Academic Account Registry (APAAR) provides a portable, verified ID for every student from school to higher‑education and skill‑training.
  • VBSA Bill 2025 (Viksit Bharat Shiksha Adhishthan)
    • Creates a single‑window apex higher‑education body with three councils (Regulatory, Accreditation, Standards).
    • Replaces fragmented UGC/AICTE/NCTE system.
  • Higher‑Education Landscape (FY 25)
    • HEIs: 70,018 institutions (up from 51,534 in 2014‑15).
    • Premier institutes: 23 IITs, 21 IIMs, 20 AIIMS (plus two International IIT campuses – Zanzibar & Abu Dhabi).
    • National Credit Framework (NCrF) adopted by 170 universities; Academic Bank of Credit serves 2,660 HEIs with 4.6 crore APAAR IDs created (2.2 crore with credit).
  • School‑level Initiatives
    • PM SHRI (Schools‑with‑Holistic‑Transformation‑and‑Inclusion) – 13,076 schools (≈ 52.8 % of government‑aided schools) equipped with ICT, smart‑classrooms, labs, sports councils, “Jadui Pitara”.
    • Kasturba Gandhi Balika Vidyalayas (KGBVs) – 2,682 KGBVs (≈ 44 % upgraded by 31 Mar 2025) providing residential schooling for girls in Educationally Backward Blocks.
    • Co‑location of Anganwadi Centres (AWC) with schools – 299,544 AWC‑school pairs (32.9 % of villages).
  • Adult‑Literacy & Lifelong‑Learning
    • ULLAS (Universal Literacy & Learning for Adults) – 3.1 crore learners, 47 lakh volunteer teachers, 1.7 crore neo‑literates (full literacy achieved in Ladakh, Mizoram, Goa, Tripura, Himachal Pradesh).
  • Performance‑Assessment – PARAKH 2024
    • Covered 21.15 lakh students (Grades 3, 6, 9), 2.70 lakh teachers, 74,000+ schools.
  • Teaching‑Force Enrichment
    • Professors of Practice (PoP) – UGC/AICTE: > 18,000 industry experts registered across 536 institutes (as of 23 Jan 2026).
    • AI & STEM Centres of Excellence – established at AIIMS‑Delhi, PGIMER‑Chandigarh, AIIMS‑Rishikesh (collaborations with Wadhwani Institute) for AI‑driven clinical decision support, TB screening, etc.
  • Skill‑Development & Training
    • Formal‑skill‑training share (PLFS): 0.97 % of 14‑18 yr youth received formal training (≈ 11.33 lakh).
    • Vocational/technical‑training share rose from 8.1 % (2017‑18) to 34.7 % (2023‑24).
    • National Credit Framework (NCrF) and e‑NAM (e‑National Agriculture Market) provide platforms for skill‑aligned credit and market access, but these belong more to agriculture; still relevant for rural education.

Direct‑Cash & Rural‑Income Support

  • Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM‑Kisan)
    • ₹6,000 per year cash transfer to ≈ 120 crore small‑and‑marginal farmers.
    • Total outlay ₹4.09 lakh cr (21 instalments, as of Aug 2025).
  • Pradhan Mantri Kisan Maandhan Yojana (PM‑KMY) – voluntary pension for cultivators, ₹6,000 per year contribution; 24.92 lac enrolments (31 Mar 2025).
  • National Food Security & Nutrition Mission (NFSNM) (formerly NFSM) – continues to boost productivity of rice, wheat, pulses, coarse cereals and commercial/nutri‑cereals; integrated with RKVY‑SAME.
  • Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana (PMKSY) – gross irrigated‑area share rose from 41.7 % (2001‑02) to 55.8 % (2022‑23).
  • One Nation One Ration Card (ONORC) – ≈ 80 crore beneficiaries covered across all 36 states/UTs; 99.8 % of ration cards and 98.9 % of beneficiaries linked to Aadhaar.
  • Direct‑Benefit‑Transfer (DBT) in PDS – FY 24: ₹267.6 cr transferred to > 10 lac beneficiaries in select regions; substantially reduced leakages.

Banking & Financial‑Inclusion

  • Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana (PMJDY) – 55.02 crore accounts (36.63 crore rural).
  • Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojana (PMMY) – ₹36.18 lakh cr disbursed; 55.45 crore loan accounts; > 10 crore first‑time borrowers (Oct 2025).
  • PM Street‑Vendor Atmanirbhar Nidhi (PM SVANidhi) – 5,130 beneficiaries (99 ULBs, FY 25); 33 % women, 45 % OBC, 17 % SC, 2 % ST.
    • Reported outcomes: +20 % income growth (FY 23‑25); housing upgrade 39 %, better food access 55 %, affordable health 44 %, education improvement 50 %.
    • NPA < 10 % for the scheme; PM SVANidhi NPA ≈ 9 %.

Nutrition & Behaviour‑Change

  • Cash‑Plus Model – Rajasthan (Box XI.9) – combines unconditional cash transfers with social‑behaviour‑change communication for maternal & child nutrition.
    • Impact (2022‑25): women using cash for nutrition ↑ 54 %, cash for food ↑ 30 %, women’s weight gain ↑ 35 %, dietary diversity ↑ 49 %, myth‑taboo reduction ↓ 35 %, male awareness ↑ 18 % (to 62 %).

Health‑Insurance (Agricultural)

  • Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) – 4.19 crore farmers insured (FY 24‑25), covering 6.2 crore ha (↑ 20 %).
    • Total claims paid ₹1.90 lakh cr since FY 16‑17.

Pension & Social‑Security

  • National Pension System (NPS) – 211.7 lakh subscribers (31 Dec 2025); ₹16.1 lakh cr assets under management (AUM).
    • Subscriber CAGR: 9.5 % (FY 15‑25); AUM CAGR: 37.3 %.
  • Atal Pension Yojana (APY) – subscription CAGR 43.7 % (since 2016); AUM CAGR 64.5 %.
  • Unified Pension Scheme (UPS) – launched 24 Jan 2025 for central‑government employees (minimum guaranteed pension with investment‑linked growth).
  • Social‑Security Coverage – Across all 22 central‑government schemes, coverage expanded from 22 % (FY 16) to 64.3 % (FY 25) of eligible population.

Insurance‑Sector Reforms

  • Sabka Bima Sabki Suraksha Act 2025 – raised FDI limit in insurance from 74 % → 100 %; eased share‑transfer ceiling for paid‑up equity (1 % → 5 %); increased penalties for violations (₹1 cr → ₹10 cr); created Policyholders’ Education & Protection Fund.

Rural & Urban Infrastructure Supporting Welfare
