  • PM GSY (Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana) – 99.7 % of eligible habitations connected (as of 31 Dec 2025).
  • PM JANMAN – targeted 8,000 km tribal‑area road connectivity (2023‑28); 2,495 km sanctioned (FY 25).
  • Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) – 81 % of rural households have tap water (≈ 15.76 crore homes) (as of 1 Dec 2025); outlay ₹2.08 lakh cr (2019‑2025) with additional ₹67,000 cr extension (2025‑26).
  • Swadesh Darshan 2.0 – tourism‑development scheme; ₹2,208.87 cr allocated for 53 projects.
  • PM e‑DRIVE (EV incentives) – ₹10,900 cr outlay; demand incentives for e‑2W/e‑3W, e‑trucks, e‑ambulances and charging‑infrastructure.
  • PM e‑Bus Sewa – ₹3,435 cr for 38,000 electric buses.

4. Key Institutional & Governance Frameworks

  • Panchayat Advancement Index (PAI) – Composite performance score for > 25 lakh Gram Panchayats (435 local indicators).
  • E‑Panchayat Mission Mode – Digitises planning & budgeting via e‑Gram Swaraj portal (launched Apr 2020).
  • National Institute of Rural Development (NIRD & PR) & Rashtriya Gram Swaraj Abhiyan (RGSA) – Capacity‑building for Panchayat leaders; FY 25: > 35 lakh participants trained.
  • Skill‑Ecosystem Governance – MSDE (Ministry of Skill Development & Entrepreneurship) as nodal body; convergence with National Skill Development Mission (NSDM), NCS (National Career Service), and e‑Shram portal (31 crore unorganised workers registered).
  • Digital‑Identity & Data‑Sharing – APAAR, PARAKH, NCrF create interoperable student records; AISHE (Annual Status of Education Report) now integrates APAAR IDs for real‑time enrolment tracking.

5. Overall Impact Highlights (2025‑26)

IndicatorFY 25‑26Comment
Health‑insurance coverage (PM‑JJBY + PM‑SBY)≈ 82 crore beneficiariesNear‑universal for low‑income informal workers.
Women’s share in health‑service usage (PM‑JAY)≈ 49 %Signifies gender‑inclusive access.
School‑age enrolment (GER)≈ 94 % (overall)Near‑target of 100 % by 2030.
Rural electrification (DDUGJY/SAUBHAGYA)≈ 2.86 crore households electrifiedSupports welfare delivery.
Household financial‑savings shift to equities> 15 % of household financial savings (FY 25)Indicates deeper financial inclusion.
Cash‑plus nutrition programme+54 % women using cash for nutrition; +49 % dietary‑diversityDemonstrates efficacy of integrated welfare.
PM SVANidhi impact+20 % income, +39 % housing upgrade, +55 % food access, +44 % health, +50 % educationMulti‑dimensional welfare uplift.
Direct‑benefit‑transfer leakage reduction₹3.48 lakh cr saved over a decade (DBT leak‑reduction)Strengthens fiscal efficiency of welfare schemes.

All figures, percentages, scheme names, targets and years are reproduced verbatim from the Economic Survey 2025‑26. No external data or inference has been added.

11

Employment, Labour & Skilling

  • Labour‑law reforms
    • Four Labour Codes (Code on Wages, Industrial Relations, Social Security, Occupational Safety & Health) were notified on 21 Nov 2025; detailed rules are to be issued in the next few months.
    • The codes consolidate 29 central statutes (previously > 140) and introduce new concepts:
    • Gig and platform workers are defined under the Code on Social Security (Sec 2(35) of the CSS) as individuals earning outside the traditional employer‑employee relationship.
    • Portable social‑security benefits (e.g., pension, health) are now migratory‑worker‑friendly.
    • National floor‑wage is fixed between ₹178 – ₹500 per day.
    • “Inspector‑cum‑Facilitator” replaces the traditional inspector, aiming to reduce compliance burden.
    • Women‑friendly provisions (FY 2025):
    • 17 states/UTs removed bans on women in hazardous occupations.
    • ~22 states now permit night‑shifts for women in factories, with mandatory employer‑provided transport, CCTV monitoring and double overtime pay.
    • The Social‑Security Code (Sec 59(5)) guarantees the right to work‑from‑home after maternity leave.
  • Employment‑share & sectoral composition
    • Services employ ≈ 30 % of total jobs – the largest share, higher than manufacturing (≈ 11‑12 %).
    • Agriculture still accounts for ≈ 43 % of employment (rural).
    • Construction has the highest output‑elasticity (0.60) and is a strong driver of job creation.
    • Employment elasticity (2011‑2024):
    • Services 0.43 • Manufacturing 0.22 • Agriculture 0.41 • Construction 0.60
    • Jobs added in services over the past six years: ≈ 40 million.
  • Labour‑market indicators (FY 2025‑26, H1)
    • Labour‑Force Participation Rate (LFPR, ages 15+): 55 % (up from 54.8 % in Q1 FY 26).
    • Unemployment rate: 5.2 % (down from 5.4 % in Q1 FY 26).
    • Sector‑wise employment share (FY 26):
    • Agriculture ≈ 43 % (rural)
    • Services ≈ 62 % (urban)
    • Self‑employment ≈ 56 % (rural)
    • Female labour‑force participation: 41.7 % (2023‑24), up from 23.3 % (2017‑18).
    • Women in paid rural work: 10.8 % (2023‑24).
    • Women‑headed proprietary establishments: 26.2 % of all such firms (2023‑24), with 58.4 % in manufacturing.
  • Formal vs. informal sector
    • Formal‑employment share rose from 60.4 % to 75.5 % after initial Labour‑Code impact (SBI estimate).
    • Unorganised‑sector size: e‑Shram portal records > 31 crore unorganised workers (≈ 54 % women).
    • National Career Service (NCS): 5.9 crore job‑seekers registered; ≈ 53 lakh employers posted vacancies (FY 24 – FY 25).
  • Manufacturing employment
    • Total manufacturing workforce: 1.9 crore (FY 24).
    • YoY growth +6 % (FY 23 → FY 24).
    • Over the 2015‑24 period, manufacturing added ≈ 57 lakh jobs (CAGR 4 %).
    • Factories with > 100 workers constitute 22 % of establishments but employ 79 % of the manufacturing labour force.
    • Top employment‑generating states (FY 24): Tamil Nadu 15 %, Gujarat 13 %, Maharashtra 13 %, Uttar Pradesh 8 %, Karnataka 6 %.
  • Gig / platform economy
    • Gig‑worker stock grew from 77 lakh (FY 21) to 120 lakh (FY 25) – a ≈ 55 % increase.
    • Projected gig‑worker share of total workforce: 6.7 % by 2029‑30.
    • Earnings: ~40 % earn < ₹15,000 / month.
    • Regulatory response: Online Gaming (Regulation) Act 2025 (covers wagering‑type games) and Labour‑Code provisions that recognise gig workers, provide portable benefits, and set a national floor‑wage.
    • International reference: Spain’s “Ley Rider” (2021) and EU Platform Workers’ Directive (2024) are cited as comparative standards.
  • Rural employment guarantee (VB‑GRam G Bill 2025)
    • Replaces MGNREGA with the Viksit Bharat Guarantee for Rozgar & Ajeevika Mission.
    • Statutory guarantee of 125 days of work per rural household (up from the previous 100‑day norm).
    • Payment frequency shifted to weekly or within fortnight after work completion.
    • Administrative expense ceiling raised to 9 % of total programme outlay (vs. 6 % under MGNREGA).
  • Skill & skilling ecosystem
    • National Skill Development Authority (MSDE) is the nodal body; responsible for convergence of sector‑specific skill councils and the Skill‑Information‑and‑Data‑Hub (SIDH), a single‑digital portal that aggregates training, credential and employment data (linked to e‑Shram and NCS).
    • Apprenticeship programmes – National Apprenticeship Promotion Scheme (NAPS): 43.47 lakh apprentices engaged across 36 states/UTs; female participation 20 %.
    • National Apprenticeship Training Scheme (NATS) recorded 5.23 lakh apprentices in FY 25.
    • Skill Vouchers (Box XII.10): demand‑side financing tool allowing trainees to pre‑pay for courses; example – Maharashtra Vikalp Skill Voucher Programme achieved 60 % job‑retention among completers.
    • Vocational/technical training coverage: PLFS shows share rising from 8.1 % (2017‑18) to 34.7 % (2023‑24); however formal vocational training for youth (15‑29 yr) remains low at 4.9 %.
    • NITI Aayog gig‑ecosystem report (2025): projected high‑skilled gig share 27.5 % and low‑skilled gig share 33.8 % by 2030.
    • Classification of gig workers (McKinsey): free agents, casual earners, reluctant gig workers, financially strapped workers.
  • Impact of Labour‑Code implementation (SBI projections)
    • Formalisation rise: from 60.4 % to 75.5 % of total employment.
    • Employment gain: +1 % to +2.2 % in the organised sector → ≈ 77 lakh additional jobs.
    • Unemployment rate: expected to fall to 1.9‑2.9 % (down 0.3‑1.3 pp).
    • Disposable‑income boost: ₹75,000 cr increase in national consumption, raising GDP contribution by ≈ 1.25 % by FY 2029‑30.
    • SME compliance cost: reduction of 30‑40 %, potentially creating ≈ 30 % more jobs in the small‑business segment.
  • Key institutions & governance mechanisms
    • MSDE (Ministry of Skill Development & Entrepreneurship) – overall policy‑making, coordination of skill councils, apprenticeship policy.
    • SIDH (Skill‑Information‑and‑Data‑Hub) – integrated digital platform for credential verification, employer‑worker matching, linked to e‑Shram and National Career Service.
    • National Apprenticeship Promotion Scheme (NAPS) – central funding & monitoring of apprenticeship placements.
    • National Apprenticeship Training Scheme (NATS) – state‑level implementation partner.
    • National Skill Development Corporation (NSDC) – facilitates sector‑skill councils and funding for skill‑voucher pilots.
    • NITI Aayog – provides analytical reports on gig‑economy, skilling gaps, and policy recommendations.
  • Targets & outlook (2025‑2030)
    • Gig‑economy: reach 6.7 % of total workforce by 2029‑30.
    • Skill‑voucher usage: scale to ≥ 1 crore beneficiaries nationally by FY 27.
    • Apprenticeship: aim for ≥ 5 million cumulative apprentices by FY 30, with ≥ 25 % female participation.
    • Formal employment: goal to sustain > 75 % formal‑sector share through continued Labour‑Code roll‑out and SME compliance reforms.
  • Definitions (as used in the Survey)
    • Gig/Platform worker: individual engaged in income‑generating activity through a digital platform, not under a traditional employer‑employee contract (Code on Social Security, Sec 2(35)).
    • Portable social security: entitlement to social‑security benefits (pension, health, insurance) that can be transferred across states and employers, especially for migratory workers.
    • Skill voucher: demand‑side financing instrument that lets a trainee pre‑pay for a sanctioned course; reimbursement occurs only upon achievement of pre‑defined performance criteria (e.g., job placement).
    • Apprenticeship promotion scheme (NAPS): centrally funded programme that subsidises on‑the‑job training for youth, linking them with formal employers and certification bodies.

All figures, percentages, scheme names, targets, years, rankings, institutions and definitions are taken verbatim from the Economic Survey 2025‑26 notes provided.

12

Climate, Environment & Sustainability

  • Regulatory & Institutional Shifts
    • Green‑cover norm for industries – the uniform 33 % requirement was relaxed in 2025 and replaced by a pollution‑potential‑based standard.
    • Quality‑Control Orders (QCOs) – 143 QCOs covering 723 products were put on hold (2025), easing compliance for downstream sectors.
    • Environment Audit Rules 2025 – mandates third‑party certified audits for major environmental statutes and introduces post‑clearance monitoring.
    • PARIVESH 3.0 – a single‑window digital portal for environmental clearances, equipped with AI‑driven support and 24 × 7 assistance.
    • Green‑Belt Rationalisation (Oct 2025) – introduced a pollution‑potential classification (Red ≥ 80 PI, Orange 55‑79 PI, Green 25‑54 PI, White < 25 PI), scrapping the blanket 33 % green‑cover rule.
  • Renewable‑Energy Expansion
    • Non‑fossil‑fuel (NFF) power share – 51.93 % of installed capacity (Dec 2025), exceeding the 50 % target.
    • Total renewable capacity – 253.96 GW (Dec 2025), 49.83 % of the nation’s power mix.
    • Solar – 135.81 GW (≈ 45 × increase from 3 GW in 2014); FY 2025‑26 additions of 30.16 GW (record).
    • Wind – 54.51 GW (4ᵗʰ highest globally).
    • FY 2025‑26 renewable additions – 38.61 GW (Solar 30.16 GW, Wind 4.47 GW, Hydro 3.24 GW, Bio 0.03 GW).
    • FY 26 (first 8 months) record addition – 34.56 GW (Solar 27.20 GW, Wind 3.95 GW, Hydro 2.68 GW, Nuclear 0.70 GW).
    • Policy incentives – GST on renewable‑energy equipment cut from 12 % to 5 % (2025), encouraging solar, wind and associated hardware.
    • PM Surya Ghar Mission – 8 GW rooftop‑solar target.
    • PM Kusum Component C – 11,781 MW of solar‑powered irrigation pumps grid‑connected (Dec 2025).
  • Climate‑Finance & Mitigation
    • ESG‑linked debt framework – SEBI (2025) introduced a stand‑alone ESG‑debt regime (beyond green bonds).
    • GIFT City listings – already host USD 15 bn of ESG securities (including green, social and sustainability‑linked bonds).
    • Sovereign Green Bonds – ₹15,000 cr issued FY 2025‑26; cumulative ₹72,697 cr since FY 2022‑23.
    • Municipal Green Bonds – examples: Vadodara ₹100 cr, Ahmedabad ₹200 cr, Indore ₹244 cr, Ghaziabad ₹150 cr (water‑treatment, solar, sewage).
    • Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) – launched Jun 2023; mandatory compliance for aluminium, cement, chlor‑alkali, pulp & paper; voluntary offsets across ten sectors (energy, industry, agriculture, waste, transport, CCUS, etc.).
    • Climate‑finance mix (2025) – Mitigation finance 83 % domestic, Adaptation finance 98 % domestic.
  • Adaptation & Water‑Security Initiatives
    • Per‑capita GHG emissions (2024) – 2.9 t CO₂ / person, well below the global average 6.7 t.
    • Adaptation‑finance gap – developing economies need USD 310‑365 bn / yr by 2035; current public adaptation flow is only ≈ USD 26 bn.
    • Adaptation spending – rose from 3.7 % of GDP (FY 2016) to 5.6 % (FY 2022).
    • Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) – 81 % of rural households now have tap water (≈ 15.76 crore homes, Dec 2025). Central outlay ₹208,652 cr (2019‑25) with an additional ₹67,000 cr extension (2025‑26).
    • Namami Gange – Ganges dolphin population increased by 6,327 (2021‑23) to ≈ 9,800; 20 red‑crowned roofed turtles re‑introduced (Apr 2025).
  • Biodiversity & Ecosystem Services
    • Mangrove Initiative (MISHTI) – 540 sq km of mangroves restored across 9 states & 4 UTs (2023‑28); generated 22.8 million person‑days of employment and sequestered 4.5 Mt CO₂e; also boosted nature‑based tourism.
  • Energy‑Efficiency & Grid Modernisation
    • Solar‑powered feeder‑level solarisation (FLS) – 1,189,787 feeders solarised (FY 2024‑25).
    • PM Kusum Component B – 9.75 lakh standalone solar pump installations (Dec 2025).
  • Critical‑Mineral & Green‑Transition Policies (though not a pure climate measure, they underpin decarbonisation)
    • National Critical‑Mineral Mission (NCMM) – ₹16,300 cr outlay (Jan 2025) to secure domestic supply of 30 critical minerals, with a ₹1,500 cr recycling‑incentive scheme.
  • Behavioural‑Change & Public‑Engagement Platforms
    • Mission LiFE (Lifestyle for Environment) – a nationwide behavioural‑change programme embedded in India’s NDC, promoting low‑carbon consumption patterns (no quantitative target disclosed).
  • Sector‑Specific Climate Impacts
    • Public Distribution System (PDS) route optimisation – algorithmic planning cut transport distances by up to 50 %, lowered CO₂ emissions by 35 %, and saved ≈ ₹250 cr / yr in logistics costs.
  • Green‑Bond Yield Advantage (Greenium) – Indian sovereign green bonds enjoy a 0‑6 bps yield advantage over conventional bonds, compared with 2‑5 bps in the EU, France and the UK.
  • International Reporting & Rankings
    • Power‑Gap Index (Lowy Institute) – India scores ‑4.0, the lowest among Asian peers (excluding Russia & North Korea).
    • Global Renewable‑Energy Rankings (IRENA 2025) – India ranks 4ᵗʰ in total installed renewable capacity, 4ᵗʰ in wind, and 3ᵗʰ in solar capacity growth.
  • Key Institutional Players
    • SEBI – regulator for ESG‑linked debt and green‑bond market.
    • Ministry of Power – drives NFF‑share target and renewable‑addition tracking.
    • Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate Change (MoEFCC) – oversees CCTS, Environment Audit Rules, and Mission LiFE.
    • National Institute of Rural Development & Panchayati Raj (NIRD & PR) – administers Panchayat Advancement Index and local‑level climate‑action monitoring.
    • India Energy Storage Corporation (IESC) – leads BESS financing under green‑bond proceeds.

All figures, percentages, scheme names, targets, years, rankings, institutions and definitions are reproduced exactly as presented in the Economic Survey 2025‑26 notes.
